narrative:
With these words I was thinking that I had made an end of the discussion; but the end, in truth, proved to be only a beginning. For Glaucon, who is always the most pugnacious of men, was dissatisfied at Thrasymachus' retirement; he wanted to have the battle out.

So he said to me: "Socrates, do you wish really to persuade us, or only to seem to have persuaded us, that to be just is always better than to be unjust?"
refashioned:
I thought the debate was over -- but it turned out to be just getting started. Glaucon, who never backs down from an argument, was unhappy that Thrasymachus had given up so easily. He wanted the fight to continue.

"Socrates," he said to me, "do you actually want to convince us that being just is always better than being unjust -- or do you just want to appear to have convinced us?"
elegant: I thought the discussion was finished, but Glaucon -- always a fighter -- was unsatisfied with Thrasymachus's surrender. He pressed me: "Do you truly want to persuade us that justice is always better than injustice, or merely appear to have done so?"
summary: After Thrasymachus concedes defeat in Book I, Glaucon reopens the debate, challenging Socrates to genuinely prove -- not just seem to prove -- that justice is always preferable to injustice.
distilled: Glaucon challenges Socrates to truly demonstrate, not merely appear to demonstrate, that justice is always superior to injustice.
notes: Socrates narrates; Glaucon speaks -- Glaucon is Plato's older brother -- Continues directly from the end of Book I where Thrasymachus argued that injustice is more profitable than justice -- The distinction between 'really persuading' and 'seeming to persuade' foreshadows the appearance-vs-reality theme central to Book II -- Thrasymachus: the sophist from Book I who argued justice is 'the advantage of the stronger'

narrative:
"I should wish really to persuade you," I replied, "if I could."

"Then you certainly have not succeeded," Glaucon said. "Let me ask you now: How would you arrange goods -- are there not some which we welcome for their own sakes, and independently of their consequences, as, for example, harmless pleasures and enjoyments, which delight us at the time, although nothing follows from them?"

"I agree in thinking that there is such a class," I replied.

"Is there not also a second class of goods, such as knowledge, sight, health, which are desirable not only in themselves, but also for their results?"

"Certainly," I said.

"And would you not recognize a third class, such as gymnastic, and the care of the sick, and the physician's art; also the various ways of money-making -- these do us good but we regard them as disagreeable; and no one would choose them for their own sakes, but only for the sake of some reward or result which flows from them?"

"There is," I said, "this third class also. But why do you ask?"

"Because I want to know in which of the three classes you would place justice?"

"In the highest class," I replied, "among those goods which he who would be happy desires both for their own sake and for the sake of their results."

"Then the many are of another mind; they think that justice is to be reckoned in the troublesome class, among goods which are to be pursued for the sake of rewards and of reputation, but in themselves are disagreeable and rather to be avoided."

"I know," I said, "that this is their manner of thinking, and that this was the thesis which Thrasymachus was maintaining just now, when he censured justice and praised injustice. But I am too stupid to be convinced by him."
refashioned:
"I would genuinely like to persuade you," I replied, "if I could."

"Well, you haven't," Glaucon said. "Let me ask you something. How would you classify different kinds of goods? Aren't there some things we value purely for their own sake -- simple pleasures and enjoyments that are delightful in the moment, even if nothing comes of them?"

"Yes, I agree there is such a category," I said.

"And isn't there a second category -- things like knowledge, sight, and health -- that are valuable both in themselves and for what they produce?"

"Certainly."

"And wouldn't you also recognize a third category -- things like exercise, medical treatment, and various ways of making money? These benefit us, but we find them unpleasant. No one would choose them for their own sake, only for the rewards they bring."

"Yes, there is this third category too. But why do you ask?"

"Because I want to know: in which of these three categories would you place justice?"

"In the highest," I replied -- "among those goods that anyone who wants to be happy values both for their own sake and for their results."

"Most people disagree with you. They put justice in the unpleasant category -- something to be endured for the sake of rewards and reputation, but inherently disagreeable."

"I know that is what people think," I said. "It is exactly what Thrasymachus was arguing when he attacked justice and praised injustice. But I am not convinced by him."
elegant: Glaucon establishes a three-part classification of goods: (1) things valued purely for themselves, like simple pleasures; (2) things valued both for themselves and their consequences, like knowledge and health; (3) things valued only for their consequences, like exercise or money-making. Socrates places justice in the highest class -- valued both intrinsically and for its results. Glaucon counters that most people place justice in the third, disagreeable class, endured only for reputation and rewards.
summary: Glaucon classifies goods into three types: valued for themselves alone, valued both intrinsically and for results, or valued only for results despite being unpleasant. Socrates places justice in the second, highest class, but Glaucon notes most people consider justice merely a necessary burden endured for its rewards.
distilled: Socrates considers justice intrinsically and instrumentally good, but most people view it as an unpleasant necessity endured only for external rewards.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The three-fold classification of goods is original to this passage and becomes the framework for the entire challenge -- Thrasymachus's position from Book I (338c): justice is the advantage of the stronger -- This taxonomy anticipates Aristotle's later classification of goods in the Nicomachean Ethics -- The 'highest class' (second category) becomes the standard Socrates must meet: proving justice is good both in itself and in its consequences

narrative:
"I wish," Glaucon said, "that you would hear me as well as him, and then I shall see whether you and I agree. For Thrasymachus seems to me, like a snake, to have been charmed by your voice sooner than he ought to have been; but to my mind the nature of justice and injustice have not yet been made clear. Setting aside their rewards and results, I want to know what they are in themselves, and how they inwardly work in the soul.

"If you please, then, I will revive the argument of Thrasymachus. And first I will speak of the nature and origin of justice according to the common view of them. Secondly, I will show that all men who practise justice do so against their will, of necessity, but not as a good. And thirdly, I will argue that there is reason in this view, for the life of the unjust is after all better far than the life of the just -- if what they say is true, Socrates, since I myself am not of their opinion.

"But still I acknowledge that I am perplexed when I hear the voices of Thrasymachus and myriads of others dinning in my ears; and, on the other hand, I have never yet heard the superiority of justice to injustice maintained by any one in a satisfactory way. I want to hear justice praised in respect of itself; then I shall be satisfied, and you are the person from whom I think that I am most likely to hear this; and therefore I will praise the unjust life to the utmost of my power, and my manner of speaking will indicate the manner in which I desire to hear you too praising justice and censuring injustice. Will you say whether you approve of my proposal?"

"Indeed I do," I replied; "nor can I imagine any theme about which a man of sense would oftener wish to converse."

"I am delighted," he replied, "to hear you say so, and shall begin by speaking, as I proposed, of the nature and origin of justice."
refashioned:
"I wish you would hear my argument too," Glaucon said, "and then we will see if we agree. Thrasymachus, it seems to me, was like a snake charmed by your voice -- he gave in too easily. The true nature of justice and injustice still has not been made clear. Setting aside rewards and consequences entirely, I want to know what justice and injustice are in themselves, and what they do to the soul from within.

"So let me revive Thrasymachus's argument. First, I will lay out the common account of what justice is and where it comes from. Second, I will show that everyone who practices justice does so unwillingly -- out of necessity, not because they see it as a good. Third, I will argue that this view makes sense, because the unjust life really is better than the just life -- if the common view is correct. I do not personally believe this, Socrates.

"But I confess I am troubled. The arguments of Thrasymachus and countless others ring in my ears, and I have never heard anyone defend the superiority of justice over injustice in a way that truly satisfies. I want to hear justice praised for what it is in itself. You are the one most likely to give me that defense. So I will argue for injustice as powerfully as I can, and the way I argue will show you exactly how I want to hear you argue for justice. Do you accept this proposal?"

"Absolutely," I said. "I cannot think of a topic more worthy of serious conversation."

"Wonderful," he replied. "Then let me begin, as I said, with the nature and origin of justice."
elegant: Glaucon declares Thrasymachus surrendered too easily -- like a snake charmed by Socrates' voice. He wants to know what justice and injustice are in themselves, apart from all consequences. He proposes a three-part challenge: first, the social-contract origin of justice; second, that people practice justice only from necessity; third, that the unjust life is rationally preferable. He will argue for injustice at full strength so Socrates can see exactly what kind of defense justice requires. Socrates eagerly accepts.
summary: Glaucon announces he will play devil's advocate, arguing the case for injustice as powerfully as possible, so that Socrates will be forced to defend justice purely on its own merits -- stripped of all rewards, reputation, and consequences. He outlines a three-part challenge covering the origin, practice, and rationality of justice.
distilled: Glaucon will steel-man the case for injustice in three parts, demanding Socrates prove justice is good in itself, apart from all external rewards.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- Glaucon explicitly disclaims belief in the position he will argue (devil's advocate / steel-manning) -- The snake-charming metaphor for Thrasymachus echoes the sophist-as-serpent trope -- 'What they are in themselves and how they inwardly work in the soul' sets the standard for the rest of the Republic -- Glaucon's three-part structure: (1) social contract theory of justice (2.4), (2) Ring of Gyges thought experiment (2.5-2.7), (3) comparison of just and unjust lives (2.8-2.11) -- Pindar and Archilochus will be cited later -- This challenge drives the entire Republic: Books II-X are Socrates' response

narrative:
Glaucon spoke: "They say that to do injustice is, by nature, good; to suffer injustice, evil; but that the evil is greater than the good. And so when men have both done and suffered injustice and have had experience of both, not being able to avoid the one and obtain the other, they think that they had better agree among themselves to have neither; hence there arise laws and mutual covenants; and that which is ordained by law is termed by them lawful and just.

"This they affirm to be the origin and nature of justice -- it is a mean or compromise, between the best of all, which is to do injustice and not be punished, and the worst of all, which is to suffer injustice without the power of retaliation; and justice, being at a middle point between the two, is tolerated not as a good, but as the lesser evil, and honoured by reason of the inability of men to do injustice. For no man who is worthy to be called a man would ever submit to such an agreement if he were able to resist; he would be mad if he did. Such is the received account, Socrates, of the nature and origin of justice."
refashioned:
Glaucon continued: "According to the common view, doing injustice is naturally good and suffering injustice is naturally bad -- but suffering it is worse than doing it is good. So when people have experienced both sides, and realize they cannot reliably do injustice without also suffering it, they agree among themselves to neither commit nor endure it. This is where laws and social contracts come from. What the law commands, they call 'just.'

"This, they say, is the origin and nature of justice: a compromise between the ideal scenario -- committing injustice with impunity -- and the worst scenario -- suffering injustice with no means of retaliation. Justice sits in the middle, tolerated not because it is genuinely good, but because it is the lesser evil. People honor it only because they lack the power to be unjust successfully. Any person truly capable of getting away with injustice would be a fool to accept such an agreement. That, Socrates, is the standard account of what justice is and where it comes from."
elegant: The common account holds that doing injustice is naturally advantageous and suffering it is harmful, but the harm of suffering outweighs the benefit of doing. Unable to reliably exploit others without being exploited in return, people compromise: they agree to laws prohibiting injustice for all. Justice is thus not a genuine good but a pragmatic middle ground -- honored only because people lack the power to be unjust with impunity.
summary: Glaucon presents the social contract theory of justice: people agree to be just not because justice is good, but because it is a practical compromise between the ideal of unpunished wrongdoing and the worst fate of being wronged without recourse.
distilled: Justice originates as a social contract -- a compromise people accept only because they lack the power to commit injustice with impunity.
notes: Glaucon speaking (presenting the common view, not his own) -- This is one of the earliest formulations of social contract theory, anticipating Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau -- The 'mean or compromise' language echoes but inverts Aristotle's later doctrine of the mean -- 'No man worthy to be called a man' suggests the common view ties masculinity/strength to the willingness to be unjust -- Cross-reference: Thrasymachus's argument in Book I (338c-339a) that justice serves the stronger -- Cross-reference: Protagoras 322a-323a for an alternative social contract account

narrative:
Glaucon continued: "Now that those who practise justice do so involuntarily and because they have not the power to be unjust will best appear if we imagine something of this kind: having given both to the just and the unjust power to do what they will, let us watch and see whither desire will lead them; then we shall discover in the very act the just and unjust man to be proceeding along the same road, following their interest, which all natures deem to be their good, and are only diverted into the path of justice by the force of law.

"The liberty which we are supposing may be most completely given to them in the form of such a power as is said to have been possessed by Gyges the ancestor of Croesus the Lydian. According to the tradition, Gyges was a shepherd in the service of the king of Lydia; there was a great storm, and an earthquake made an opening in the earth at the place where he was feeding his flock. Amazed at the sight, he descended into the opening, where, among other marvels, he beheld a hollow brazen horse, having doors, at which he stooping and looking in saw a dead body of stature, as appeared to him, more than human, and having nothing on but a gold ring; this he took from the finger of the dead and reascended."
refashioned:
"To prove that people practice justice only because they lack the power to be unjust," Glaucon continued, "consider this thought experiment. Give both the just person and the unjust person complete freedom to do whatever they want, then watch where their desires lead them. You will find that both follow the exact same path -- pursuing their own self-interest. The only thing that diverts anyone onto the path of justice is the force of law.

"The kind of freedom I am describing is best illustrated by the legendary power of Gyges, ancestor of Croesus the Lydian king. The story goes that Gyges was a shepherd serving the king of Lydia. One day a violent storm struck and an earthquake tore open the ground where he was tending his flock. Astonished, he climbed down into the chasm. Among other wonders, he found a hollow bronze horse with doors. Peering inside, he saw a corpse of more-than-human size, wearing nothing but a gold ring. He took the ring from the dead man's finger and climbed back out."
elegant: To show that justice is practiced only from necessity, Glaucon proposes a thought experiment: give both a just and an unjust person total freedom, and both will pursue self-interest identically. He illustrates this with the legend of Gyges, a Lydian shepherd who discovered a chasm opened by an earthquake, descended into it, and found a giant corpse inside a bronze horse wearing nothing but a gold ring, which he took.
summary: Glaucon introduces the Ring of Gyges myth to argue that everyone -- just and unjust alike -- would behave the same way if freed from consequences. The story begins with a shepherd named Gyges discovering a mysterious ring on a superhuman corpse inside an earthquake-opened chasm.
distilled: The Ring of Gyges thought experiment: if freed from all consequences, both just and unjust people would pursue pure self-interest.
notes: Glaucon speaking -- The Ring of Gyges is one of philosophy's most famous thought experiments -- Gyges: historical figure, ancestor of Croesus, king of Lydia (c. 680-644 BCE); Herodotus tells a different version of Gyges' rise to power (Histories 1.8-13) -- Croesus: last king of Lydia, proverbially wealthy -- The 'hollow brazen horse' may echo the Trojan Horse -- The superhuman corpse suggests mythic/divine origins for the ring's power -- This passage establishes the philosophical problem of whether morality requires enforcement -- Cross-reference: Tolkien acknowledged the Ring of Gyges as an influence on the One Ring

narrative:
"Now the shepherds met together, according to custom, that they might send their monthly report about the flocks to the king; into their assembly he came having the ring on his finger, and as he was sitting among them he chanced to turn the collet of the ring inside his hand, when instantly he became invisible to the rest of the company and they began to speak of him as if he were no longer present.

"He was astonished at this, and again touching the ring he turned the collet outwards and reappeared; he made several trials of the ring, and always with the same result -- when he turned the collet inwards he became invisible, when outwards he reappeared.

"Whereupon he contrived to be chosen one of the messengers who were sent to the court; where as soon as he arrived he seduced the queen, and with her help conspired against the king and slew him, and took the kingdom.

"Suppose now that there were two such magic rings, and the just put on one of them and the unjust the other; no man can be imagined to be of such an iron nature that he would stand fast in justice. No man would keep his hands off what was not his own when he could safely take what he liked out of the market, or go into houses and lie with any one at his pleasure, or kill or release from prison whom he would, and in all respects be like a God among men.

"Then the actions of the just would be as the actions of the unjust; they would both come at last to the same point. And this we may truly affirm to be a great proof that a man is just, not willingly or because he thinks that justice is any good to him individually, but of necessity, for wherever any one thinks that he can safely be unjust, there he is unjust."
refashioned:
"At the regular monthly assembly where the shepherds reported to the king about the flocks, Gyges attended wearing the ring. While sitting among his fellows, he happened to turn the ring's setting inward, and instantly became invisible. The others spoke of him as though he had left. Astonished, he turned the setting outward and reappeared. He tested this repeatedly -- always the same result. Inward: invisible. Outward: visible.

"Once he understood the ring's power, he arranged to be selected as one of the messengers sent to the royal court. Upon arriving, he seduced the queen, conspired with her against the king, murdered him, and seized the throne.

"Now imagine there were two such rings -- one given to a just person, the other to an unjust person. No one has such iron willpower that they would remain just. Who would keep their hands off other people's property when they could take anything from the marketplace without consequence? Who would refrain from entering any house, sleeping with anyone they desired, killing or freeing anyone at will -- living, in effect, as a god among mortals?

"The just person would end up behaving exactly like the unjust person. Both would arrive at the same destination. This proves conclusively that no one is just by choice or because they believe justice benefits them personally. People are just only out of necessity. The moment anyone believes they can get away with injustice, they act unjustly."
elegant: Gyges discovers the ring grants invisibility. He uses it to seduce the queen, murder the king, and seize the throne. Glaucon's challenge: give such a ring to both a just and an unjust person, and both will behave identically -- stealing, killing, and satisfying every desire like gods among men. This proves no one is just willingly; people are just only because they fear consequences. Remove the consequences, and every person becomes unjust.
summary: Gyges discovers his ring grants invisibility, and immediately uses it to seize power through seduction and murder. Glaucon argues that anyone -- even the most just person -- given such a ring would behave identically, proving that justice is maintained only by fear of consequences, never by genuine choice.
distilled: Given the power of total impunity, even the just person would act unjustly, proving justice is practiced from necessity rather than from any belief in its inherent value.
notes: Glaucon speaking -- 'Collet': the bezel or setting of a ring -- The progression from invisibility to seduction to regicide to tyranny shows escalating corruption -- 'Like a God among men': the ring grants divine-level freedom from human accountability -- This argument is a form of psychological egoism: all humans are fundamentally self-interested -- Cross-reference: Republic Book I (344a-c), Thrasymachus's claim that perfect injustice is most profitable -- Cross-reference: Tolkien's One Ring similarly corrupts through the promise of power without accountability -- The thought experiment anticipates modern debates about surveillance, deterrence, and intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation

narrative:
Glaucon continued: "For all men believe in their hearts that injustice is far more profitable to the individual than justice, and he who argues as I have been supposing, will say that they are right.

"If you could imagine any one obtaining this power of becoming invisible, and never doing any wrong or touching what was another's, he would be thought by the lookers-on to be a most wretched idiot, although they would praise him to one another's faces, and keep up appearances with one another from a fear that they too might suffer injustice.

"Enough of this."
refashioned:
"Deep down," Glaucon continued, "everyone believes that injustice is far more profitable to the individual than justice. Those who argue as I have been arguing would say the majority is right.

"If someone obtained the power of invisibility and never used it to take what belongs to others or do any wrong, onlookers would privately consider him a pathetic fool -- even though publicly they would praise him to each other's faces, keeping up the pretense out of fear that they themselves might become victims of injustice.

"But enough on this point."
elegant: Everyone secretly believes injustice is more profitable than justice. If an invisible man never exploited his power, people would privately think him a fool -- though publicly they would praise him, maintaining the facade of valuing justice only from fear of being wronged themselves.
summary: Glaucon concludes the Ring of Gyges argument: everyone secretly believes injustice is more profitable. A person who had the power of invisibility but never exploited it would be considered a fool by others -- who would praise him publicly only out of mutual self-protection.
distilled: People praise justice publicly out of self-protective fear, while privately believing that anyone who refuses to exploit total impunity is a fool.
notes: Glaucon speaking -- The distinction between public praise and private contempt highlights the appearance-vs-reality theme -- This anticipates the 'noble lie' discussion later in Republic (414b-415d) -- The 'wretched idiot' who remains just despite impunity is the figure Socrates must ultimately vindicate -- Cross-reference: Glaucon's argument here parallels the 'immoralist challenge' found across philosophy -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (580a-583a), where Socrates finally argues the just person is genuinely happier

narrative:
"Now, if we are to form a real judgment of the life of the just and unjust, we must isolate them; there is no other way; and how is the isolation to be effected? I answer: Let the unjust man be entirely unjust, and the just man entirely just; nothing is to be taken away from either of them, and both are to be perfectly furnished for the work of their respective lives.

"First, let the unjust be like other distinguished masters of craft; like the skilful pilot or physician, who knows intuitively his own powers and keeps within their limits, and who, if he fails at any point, is able to recover himself. So let the unjust make his unjust attempts in the right way, and lie hidden if he means to be great in his injustice -- he who is found out is nobody -- for the highest reach of injustice is: to be deemed just when you are not."
refashioned:
"Now, to properly evaluate the just life against the unjust life, we need to isolate each in its purest form. There is no other way. How do we achieve this isolation? Simple: make the unjust man completely unjust and the just man completely just. Strip nothing away from either. Equip both perfectly for the lives they have chosen.

"First, the unjust man. He should be like any master craftsman -- a skilled captain or doctor who knows exactly what he is capable of, stays within those limits, and recovers quickly from any mistake. The perfectly unjust man commits injustice skillfully and stays hidden, because getting caught means you are nobody. The pinnacle of injustice is this: to be thought just when you are not."
elegant: To judge justly between the just and unjust lives, we must examine each in its pure form. The perfectly unjust person operates like a master craftsman -- skilled, self-aware, able to recover from errors. He commits injustice expertly while remaining undetected, for the supreme achievement of injustice is to be regarded as just while being anything but.
summary: Glaucon proposes a thought experiment comparing perfectly just and perfectly unjust lives in isolation. The perfectly unjust person is a master of his craft -- committing injustice skillfully while maintaining a reputation for justice, since the highest form of injustice is appearing just while being unjust.
distilled: The pinnacle of injustice is to be perfectly unjust while being universally regarded as just.
notes: Glaucon speaking -- The craft analogy (techne) is significant: Socrates frequently uses craft analogies, and here Glaucon turns the device against justice -- 'He who is found out is nobody': anticipates Machiavelli's The Prince on the necessity of appearing virtuous -- The isolation of pure types is a characteristic Platonic method (cf. the Forms) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book I (340d-341a), where Thrasymachus uses the craft analogy similarly -- The appearance/reality inversion: the perfectly unjust man appears just, the perfectly just man will appear unjust (see 2.9)

narrative:
"Therefore I say that in the perfectly unjust man we must assume the most perfect injustice; there is to be no deduction, but we must allow him, while doing the most unjust acts, to have acquired the greatest reputation for justice. If he have taken a false step he must be able to recover himself; he must be one who can speak with effect, if any of his deeds come to light, and who can force his way where force is required by his courage and strength, and command of money and friends.

"And at his side let us place the just man in his nobleness and simplicity, wishing, as Aeschylus says, to be and not to seem good. There must be no seeming, for if he seem to be just he will be honoured and rewarded, and then we shall not know whether he is just for the sake of justice or for the sake of honours and rewards; therefore, let him be clothed in justice only, and have no other covering; and he must be imagined in a state of life the opposite of the former.

"Let him be the best of men, and let him be thought the worst; then he will have been put to the proof; and we shall see whether he will be affected by the fear of infamy and its consequences. And let him continue thus to the hour of death; being just and seeming to be unjust. When both have reached the uttermost extreme, the one of justice and the other of injustice, let judgment be given which of them is the happier of the two."
refashioned:
"So the perfectly unjust man must be granted the fullest injustice with no exceptions. While committing the worst acts, he enjoys the greatest reputation for justice. If he slips up, he can recover -- he is eloquent enough to talk his way out, brave and strong enough to use force when needed, and wealthy and well-connected enough to buy his way through anything.

"Now place the just man beside him: noble and simple, wishing -- as Aeschylus puts it -- 'to be good, not merely to seem good.' He must have no appearance of justice whatsoever. If he appeared just, he would receive honor and reward, and then we could never tell whether he was just for the sake of justice itself or for the sake of those rewards. So strip him of everything except justice itself. His life must be the exact opposite of the unjust man's.

"Let him be the best of men, yet thought the worst. That is the true test. We will see whether he holds firm despite disgrace and all its consequences. Let him persist in this condition until the day he dies -- being just while appearing unjust. Once both men have reached the absolute extreme of their respective paths -- one of perfect justice, the other of perfect injustice -- let us judge which of the two is happier."
elegant: The perfectly unjust man commits the worst acts while enjoying the highest reputation for virtue -- equipped with eloquence, force, wealth, and connections to maintain his facade. Beside him stands the perfectly just man, stripped of all reputation, following Aeschylus's ideal of being rather than seeming good. He must appear wicked despite being virtuous, enduring disgrace to the hour of death. Only then can we truly judge: which life is happier?
summary: Glaucon constructs the ultimate comparison: the perfectly unjust man who appears perfectly just, versus the perfectly just man who appears perfectly unjust. By stripping justice of all external rewards and loading injustice with every advantage, the thought experiment isolates the intrinsic value of justice itself.
distilled: The true test of justice: would you choose to be genuinely just but universally condemned, over being genuinely unjust but universally admired?
notes: Glaucon speaking -- Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes (592): 'He wishes not to seem but to be the best' -- said of the character Amphiaraus -- This passage is the philosophical heart of Glaucon's challenge and sets the terms for the rest of the Republic -- The 'stripped just man' prefigures Christian imagery of the suffering righteous (cf. Job, the Passion) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (580a-588a), where Socrates finally delivers the verdict on which life is happier -- Cross-reference: Gorgias 474b-475e, where Socrates argues it is worse to do injustice than to suffer it -- The appearance/reality inversion is the central philosophical device of Book II

narrative:
"Heavens! my dear Glaucon," I said, "how energetically you polish them up for the decision, first one and then the other, as if they were two statues."

"I do my best," he said. "And now that we know what they are like there is no difficulty in tracing out the sort of life which awaits either of them. This I will proceed to describe; but as you may think the description a little too coarse, I ask you to suppose, Socrates, that the words which follow are not mine. Let me put them into the mouths of the eulogists of injustice: They will tell you that the just man who is thought unjust will be scourged, racked, bound -- will have his eyes burnt out; and, at last, after suffering every kind of evil, he will be impaled. Then he will understand that he ought to seem only, and not to be, just.

"The words of Aeschylus may be more truly spoken of the unjust than of the just. For the unjust is pursuing a reality; he does not live with a view to appearances -- he wants to be really unjust and not to seem only:"
refashioned:
"Good heavens, Glaucon," I said, "you are polishing these two figures for judgment as though they were statues at a competition!"

"I am doing my best," he replied. "Now that we have defined what each man is like, it is not hard to trace the life that awaits each of them. Let me describe it -- but since the description may seem harsh, Socrates, imagine that the following words are not mine. I am putting them in the mouths of those who champion injustice.

"They will tell you: the just man who appears unjust will be whipped, tortured, chained, blinded -- and finally, after enduring every kind of suffering, he will be impaled. Only then will he realize he should have aimed to seem just, not to actually be just.

"In fact, Aeschylus's words apply more accurately to the unjust man than the just. The unjust man pursues substance, not appearance. He does not live for how things look -- he wants to genuinely be unjust, not merely seem so."
elegant: Socrates marvels at how Glaucon polishes his two figures like statues for judgment. Glaucon then describes the fate of the perfectly just man (in the voice of injustice's advocates): he will be whipped, tortured, chained, blinded, and impaled. Only suffering will teach him he should have pursued the appearance of justice, not the reality. Ironically, Aeschylus's 'to be and not to seem' applies better to the unjust man, who pursues real injustice rather than mere appearances.
summary: Glaucon describes the terrible fate awaiting the perfectly just man who appears unjust: torture, mutilation, and death. He inverts Aeschylus's famous line, arguing that it is actually the unjust man who pursues substance over appearance, since he genuinely commits to real injustice rather than mere show.
distilled: The just man stripped of reputation faces torture and death, while the unjust man who pursues real injustice -- not mere appearance -- thrives.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The 'two statues' metaphor: Glaucon is sculpting ideal types for comparison -- 'Scourged, racked, bound, eyes burnt out, impaled': this catalog of tortures has been read as prefiguring the fate of Socrates himself (execution in 399 BCE) and by Christian readers as prefiguring the Passion of Christ -- Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes (592) again: the ironic inversion is that 'to be and not to seem' now describes the unjust man -- 'Eulogists of injustice': Glaucon distances himself from the position -- Cross-reference: Gorgias 473c-d, where Socrates discusses the suffering of the just -- The impalement reference may allude to Persian punishment practices

narrative:
Glaucon continued, quoting the poets: "His mind has a soil deep and fertile, out of which spring his prudent counsels."

"In the first place, he is thought just, and therefore bears rule in the city; he can marry whom he will, and give in marriage to whom he will; also he can trade and deal where he likes, and always to his own advantage, because he has no misgivings about injustice; and at every contest, whether in public or private, he gets the better of his antagonists, and gains at their expense, and is rich, and out of his gains he can benefit his friends, and harm his enemies; moreover, he can offer sacrifices, and dedicate gifts to the gods abundantly and magnificently, and can honour the gods or any man whom he wants to honour in a far better style than the just, and therefore he is likely to be dearer than they are to the gods.

"And thus, Socrates, gods and men are said to unite in making the life of the unjust better than the life of the just."
refashioned:
Glaucon continued, quoting the poets: "His mind is deep and fertile soil, from which wise plans spring forth."

"Because the unjust man is thought to be just, he rules the city. He marries whomever he chooses and arranges marriages for others as he pleases. He trades and deals wherever he likes, always to his own advantage, because he has no scruples about injustice. In every competition, public or private, he defeats his rivals and profits at their expense. He grows rich. From his wealth, he can reward his friends and punish his enemies. He can make lavish sacrifices and dedicate magnificent gifts to the gods, honoring them -- or any person he wishes to honor -- in a style the just man cannot match. Therefore, the gods themselves are more likely to favor him.

"And so, Socrates, both gods and men are said to conspire in making the life of the unjust man better than the life of the just."
elegant: The unjust man reaps every worldly reward: political power, advantageous marriages, profitable dealings, victory over rivals, wealth to benefit friends and harm enemies, and the ability to honor the gods with magnificent offerings. Because he can serve the gods more lavishly than the just man, even divine favor supposedly falls to him. Gods and men alike conspire to make the unjust life superior.
summary: Glaucon catalogs the rewards of the successfully unjust man: political power, wealth, advantageous relationships, victory in every contest, and even divine favor -- since his riches allow more lavish sacrifices than the just man can afford.
distilled: The unjust man who appears just gains every advantage -- political power, wealth, social dominance, and even the favor of the gods.
notes: Glaucon speaking (quoting the advocates of injustice) -- The quoted verse is from Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes (593-594), originally about Amphiaraus -- The catalog of advantages mirrors and inverts the traditional Greek ideal of the good man (agathos) -- 'Benefit friends, harm enemies': this was the conventional Greek ethical formula, critiqued by Socrates in Republic Book I (332d) and in the Crito -- The claim that the unjust man pleases the gods better through lavish sacrifice is the point Adeimantus will develop further -- Cross-reference: Republic Book I (331e-336a), Polemarchus's definition of justice as helping friends and harming enemies

narrative:
I was going to say something in answer to Glaucon, when Adeimantus, his brother, interposed.

"Socrates," he said, "you do not suppose that there is nothing more to be urged?"

"Why, what else is there?" I answered.

"The strongest point of all has not been even mentioned," he replied.

"Well, then, according to the proverb, 'Let brother help brother' -- if he fails in any part do you assist him; although I must confess that Glaucon has already said quite enough to lay me in the dust, and take from me the power of helping justice."
refashioned:
I was about to respond to Glaucon when his brother Adeimantus cut in.

"Socrates," he said, "you don't think that is everything, do you?"

"What else could there be?" I asked.

"The most powerful argument of all has not even been mentioned yet."

"Well then," I said, "as the proverb goes, 'let brother help brother.' If Glaucon has left anything out, by all means fill in the gaps -- though I must admit he has already said more than enough to knock me flat and strip me of any power to defend justice."
elegant: As Socrates prepares to respond to Glaucon, Adeimantus interrupts: the strongest argument against justice has not yet been made. Socrates, already overwhelmed by Glaucon's challenge, invites Adeimantus to press the attack further.
summary: Adeimantus, Glaucon's brother, interrupts to say the most powerful argument against justice has not yet been raised. Socrates, already feeling defeated by Glaucon's challenge, invites him to continue the assault.
distilled: Adeimantus enters the debate, claiming the strongest argument against justice has yet to be made.
notes: Socrates narrates; Adeimantus speaks -- Adeimantus is Plato's other brother (Glaucon and Adeimantus are both brothers of Plato) -- 'Let brother help brother': a Greek proverb -- Adeimantus's challenge will focus on how justice is praised (for its consequences, not itself) and how religion is weaponized to serve injustice -- Cross-reference: Adeimantus also plays a significant role in Books III-IV and VI-VII -- The transition from Glaucon to Adeimantus shifts from the theoretical challenge (Ring of Gyges, ideal types) to the cultural challenge (how society actually talks about justice)

narrative:
"Nonsense," Adeimantus replied. "But let me add something more: There is another side to Glaucon's argument about the praise and censure of justice and injustice, which is equally required in order to bring out what I believe to be his meaning.

"Parents and tutors are always telling their sons and their wards that they are to be just; but why? Not for the sake of justice, but for the sake of character and reputation; in the hope of obtaining for him who is reputed just some of those offices, marriages, and the like which Glaucon has enumerated among the advantages accruing to the unjust from the reputation of justice.

"More, however, is made of appearances by this class of persons than by the others; for they throw in the good opinion of the gods, and will tell you of a shower of benefits which the heavens, as they say, rain upon the pious; and this accords with the testimony of the noble Hesiod and Homer, the first of whom says, that the gods make the oaks of the just --

'To bear acorns at their summit, and bees in the middle; and the sheep are bowed down with their fleeces.'

And many other blessings of a like kind are provided for them. And Homer has a very similar strain; for he speaks of one whose fame is --"
refashioned:
"Nonsense," Adeimantus said. "But let me add a complementary argument. There is another dimension to Glaucon's point about how justice and injustice are praised and condemned, and it needs to be made explicit.

"Parents and teachers always tell their children and students to be just -- but why? Not for the sake of justice itself, but for the sake of reputation and social standing. They hope that appearing just will secure the very advantages Glaucon described: political offices, desirable marriages, and the like.

"But these people go even further than Glaucon's unjust man in emphasizing appearances. They invoke divine favor, claiming that the gods shower blessings on the pious. They cite Hesiod, who says the gods make the oaks of just men 'bear acorns at their crowns, and bees in their branches, and their sheep grow heavy with wool' -- along with many similar rewards. Homer strikes the same note, speaking of a ruler whose fame is --"
elegant: Adeimantus extends Glaucon's argument: even those who praise justice -- parents, teachers, poets -- do so entirely in terms of its rewards, never for its own sake. They urge children to be just for the sake of reputation, social advancement, and divine favor. Hesiod and Homer are cited as authorities who promise material blessings to the pious.
summary: Adeimantus argues that even justice's defenders undermine it: parents and teachers recommend justice solely for its reputational rewards, not its intrinsic value. They cite poets like Hesiod and Homer who promise that the gods shower material blessings on the just.
distilled: Even those who praise justice do so only for its external rewards -- reputation, social advantage, and divine favor -- never for justice itself.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- Hesiod, Works and Days (232-234): the blessings of the just city -- Homer reference continued in 2.14 -- The critique that justice is only ever praised instrumentally is the core of Adeimantus's challenge -- This argument is distinct from Glaucon's: Glaucon argued injustice is better; Adeimantus argues that even justice's advocates implicitly agree, since they only praise its consequences -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (612a-614a), where Socrates finally discusses the external rewards of justice after establishing its intrinsic value -- Hesiod and Homer: the two foundational Greek poets, authorities on morality and theology

narrative:
Adeimantus continued: "'As the fame of some blameless king who, like a god, maintains justice; to whom the black earth brings forth wheat and barley, whose trees are bowed with fruit, and his sheep never fail to bear, and the sea gives him fish.'

"Still grander are the gifts of heaven which Musaeus and his son vouchsafe to the just; they take them down into the world below, where they have the saints lying on couches at a feast, everlastingly drunk, crowned with garlands; their idea seems to be that an immortality of drunkenness is the highest meed of virtue.

"Some extend their rewards yet further; the posterity, as they say, of the faithful and just shall survive to the third and fourth generation.

"This is the style in which they praise justice. But about the wicked there is another strain; they bury them in a slough in Hades, and make them carry water in a sieve; also while they are yet living they bring them to infamy, and inflict upon them the punishments which Glaucon described as the portion of the just who are reputed to be unjust; nothing else does their invention supply.

"Such is their manner of praising the one and censuring the other."
refashioned:
Adeimantus continued: "Homer speaks of the blameless king 'who, like a god, upholds justice -- to whom the dark earth yields wheat and barley, whose trees hang heavy with fruit, whose flocks never fail, and the sea provides fish.'

"But the poets Musaeus and his son promise even grander rewards: they escort the just down to the underworld, where the blessed recline on couches at an eternal feast, perpetually drunk, crowned with garlands. Apparently, the highest reward for virtue is an eternity of drunkenness.

"Others extend the payoff further still: the descendants of the just, they say, will prosper to the third and fourth generation.

"That is how they praise justice. As for the wicked, the treatment is the reverse: they are buried in a swamp in Hades and forced to carry water in a sieve. While still alive, they suffer the same punishments Glaucon described as the fate of the just man who appears unjust -- disgrace, torture, and death. The poets have nothing more creative to offer.

"Such is their manner of praising justice and condemning injustice."
elegant: Homer promises the just king abundant harvests and flocks. Musaeus and his son go further, offering the righteous an eternity of drunken feasting in the underworld -- as if virtue's highest reward were perpetual intoxication. The wicked, meanwhile, are buried in swamps in Hades and forced to carry water in sieves. All praise of justice reduces to promised rewards; all censure of injustice reduces to threatened punishments.
summary: Adeimantus catalogs how poets reward justice and punish injustice: Homer promises material abundance, Musaeus promises eternal drunken feasting in the afterlife, and the wicked are condemned to futile torments in Hades. In every case, justice is praised only through its external consequences, never for itself.
distilled: Traditional praise of justice amounts to nothing more than promising rewards to the just and threatening punishments to the unjust -- never valuing justice for what it is.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- Homer, Odyssey 19.109-114: the just king whose land flourishes -- Musaeus: legendary pre-Homeric poet associated with the Eleusinian Mysteries; 'his son' is Eumolpus -- The 'immortality of drunkenness' is Adeimantus's sarcastic commentary on the Orphic/mystery religion conception of the afterlife -- 'Water in a sieve': the punishment of the Danaids in Hades -- 'Third and fourth generation': echoes biblical language (Exodus 20:5-6) but is from Greek tradition -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (614b-621d), the Myth of Er, where Socrates gives his own account of afterlife rewards and punishments -- Cross-reference: Phaedo 69c on mystery initiations

narrative:
"Once more, Socrates, I will ask you to consider another way of speaking about justice and injustice, which is not confined to the poets, but is found in prose writers. The universal voice of mankind is always declaring that justice and virtue are honourable, but grievous and toilsome; and that the pleasures of vice and injustice are easy of attainment, and are only censured by law and opinion.

"They say also that honesty is for the most part less profitable than dishonesty; and they are quite ready to call wicked men happy, and to honour them both in public and private when they are rich or in any other way influential, while they despise and overlook those who may be weak and poor, even though acknowledging them to be better than the others.

"But most extraordinary of all is their mode of speaking about virtue and the gods: they say that the gods apportion calamity and misery to many good men, and good and happiness to the wicked. And mendicant prophets go to rich men's doors and persuade them that they have a power committed to them by the gods of making an atonement for a man's own or his ancestor's sins by sacrifices or charms, with rejoicings and feasts; and they promise to harm an enemy, whether just or unjust, at a small cost; with magic arts and incantations binding heaven, as they say, to execute their will.

"And the poets are the authorities to whom they appeal, now smoothing the path of vice with the words of Hesiod --

'Vice may be had in abundance without trouble; the way is smooth and her dwelling-place is near. But before virtue the gods have set toil, and a tedious and uphill road.'

Then citing Homer as a witness that the gods may be influenced by men; for he also says:"
refashioned:
"Consider yet another way that justice and injustice are discussed, Socrates -- not just by poets, but by everyday writers and common opinion. Everyone agrees that justice and virtue are honorable, but hard and unpleasant, while the pleasures of vice come easily and are condemned only by law and social convention.

"People openly say that dishonesty is usually more profitable than honesty. They are perfectly willing to call wicked men happy and to honor them -- publicly and privately -- when they are rich or powerful, while looking down on the weak and poor, even while admitting those people are morally superior.

"Most remarkable of all is what they say about virtue and the gods: that the gods assign suffering to many good people and prosperity to the wicked. Wandering prophets show up at rich men's doors claiming divine authority to atone for sins -- the client's own or their ancestors' -- through sacrifices, charms, and feasts. For a small fee, they also promise to curse any enemy, just or unjust, using magic rituals that supposedly compel the gods to do their bidding.

"They cite the poets as their authority. Hesiod smooths the path to vice: 'Vice is available in abundance without effort; the road is smooth and her dwelling is nearby. But before virtue, the gods have placed hard labor and a long uphill road.' Then they cite Homer as proof that even the gods can be swayed by human action:"
elegant: Beyond poets, common wisdom declares virtue toilsome and vice easy. People honor the rich and wicked while despising the virtuous poor. Wandering prophets sell divine atonement and curses to the highest bidder. Hesiod is cited to show vice is effortless while virtue demands toil, and Homer to prove the gods themselves can be bribed. The entire cultural apparatus -- poetry, religion, popular opinion -- teaches that injustice pays and that divine consequences can be bought off.
summary: Adeimantus broadens his critique beyond poetry to all of culture: common opinion holds that vice is easy and virtue hard, the rich wicked are honored while the virtuous poor are despised, and itinerant prophets sell divine atonement and curses. Poets like Hesiod and Homer are cited as authorities for the view that the gods can be influenced and manipulated.
distilled: The entire culture -- poets, prophets, popular opinion -- teaches that vice is easy, virtue is hard, and divine consequences can be bought off.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- Hesiod, Works and Days (287-289): vice is easy, virtue is hard -- Homer quotation continued in 2.16 -- 'Mendicant prophets': itinerant religious practitioners who sold ritual services; Plato consistently criticizes them -- 'Binding heaven': the practice of katadesmoi (binding spells/curse tablets), well-attested in ancient Greece -- This passage is one of the earliest critiques of the commercialization of religion -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-392c) continues the critique of harmful poetic representations -- Cross-reference: Laws 909b-910d, where Plato proposes legal penalties for religious charlatans -- The cultural critique here anticipates Nietzsche's analysis of conventional morality

narrative:
"'The gods, too, may be turned from their purpose; and men pray to them and avert their wrath by sacrifices and soothing entreaties, and by libations and the odour of fat, when they have sinned and transgressed.'

"And they produce a host of books written by Musaeus and Orpheus, who were children of the Moon and the Muses -- that is what they say -- according to which they perform their ritual, and persuade not only individuals, but whole cities, that expiations and atonements for sin may be made by sacrifices and amusements which fill a vacant hour, and are equally at the service of the living and the dead; the latter sort they call mysteries, and they redeem us from the pains of hell, but if we neglect them no one knows what awaits us."
refashioned:
"Homer says: 'Even the gods can be persuaded; men turn aside their anger with sacrifices, gentle prayers, libations, and the smell of burnt offerings when they have sinned and transgressed.'

"Then these prophets produce stacks of books attributed to Musaeus and Orpheus -- supposedly children of the Moon and the Muses -- and use them as the basis for their rituals. They convince not just individuals but entire cities that sins can be atoned for through sacrifices and entertainments performed in idle hours, and that these rituals work equally well for the living and the dead. The rites for the dead they call 'mysteries,' which supposedly save us from the torments of the afterlife. But if we neglect them? No one knows what horrors await."
elegant: Homer teaches that even the gods can be bribed through sacrifice and prayer. Ritual specialists produce books attributed to Orpheus and Musaeus, using them to convince whole cities that sins -- for both the living and the dead -- can be expiated through paid ceremonies. These 'mysteries' promise salvation from the afterlife's punishments, with the implicit threat that neglecting them brings unknown terrors.
summary: Adeimantus describes how religious practitioners use texts attributed to Orpheus and Musaeus to sell ritual atonement for sins, promising that sacrificial ceremonies can save both the living and the dead from punishment in the afterlife.
distilled: Religious entrepreneurs sell ritual atonement using texts attributed to Orpheus and Musaeus, promising that sins can be expiated and afterlife punishments avoided through paid ceremonies.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- Homer, Iliad 9.497-501: the gods can be turned by sacrifice -- Orpheus: legendary musician and founder of the Orphic religious movement -- Musaeus: legendary poet, sometimes called son of Orpheus or of the Moon (Selene) and the Muses -- 'Mysteries' (teletai): initiation rites, especially the Eleusinian and Orphic Mysteries -- 'Children of the Moon and the Muses': the claimed divine ancestry of Orpheus/Musaeus -- This is Plato's most sustained critique of Orphic religion -- Cross-reference: Phaedo 69c, where Socrates quotes the mystery formula 'many carry the wand but few are inspired' -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (614b-621d), Myth of Er -- The passage anticipates later critiques of religious indulgences (cf. Luther's 95 Theses)

narrative:
Adeimantus proceeded: "And now when the young hear all this said about virtue and vice, and the way in which gods and men regard them, how are their minds likely to be affected, my dear Socrates -- those of them, I mean, who are quickwitted, and, like bees on the wing, light on every flower, and from all that they hear are prone to draw conclusions as to what manner of persons they should be and in what way they should walk if they would make the best of life?

"Probably the youth will say to himself in the words of Pindar --"
refashioned:
Adeimantus continued: "Now, Socrates, when young people hear all of this about virtue and vice -- about how gods and men truly regard them -- what conclusions do you think they draw? I mean the sharp ones, the ones who flit like bees from idea to idea, gathering from everything they hear a sense of what kind of person to become and how to live if they want the best possible life.

"Such a young person would probably say to himself, echoing Pindar:"
elegant: Adeimantus asks the crucial question: when intelligent young people absorb all these cultural messages -- that virtue is unrewarded, vice is profitable, and the gods can be bribed -- what rational conclusions will they draw about how to live?
summary: Adeimantus raises the devastating practical consequence of the cultural messaging he has described: intelligent young people, hearing that virtue goes unrewarded and vice can be made safe through ritual, will rationally conclude that injustice is the better path.
distilled: When bright young people hear that virtue is punished, vice rewarded, and the gods bribable, they will rationally choose injustice.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- 'Like bees on the wing': metaphor for intellectually curious youth gathering ideas -- Pindar: the great lyric poet of Thebes (c. 518-438 BCE), cited in the next passage -- The argument shifts from describing cultural messages to their practical effect on moral development -- This is fundamentally a pedagogical argument: bad moral education produces immoral citizens -- Cross-reference: Republic Books II-III (376e-403c), where Socrates designs the education of guardians specifically to counter these problems -- Cross-reference: Protagoras 325c-326e on moral education

narrative:
"'Can I by justice or by crooked ways of deceit ascend a loftier tower which may be a fortress to me all my days?'

"For what men say is that, if I am really just and am not also thought just, profit there is none, but the pain and loss on the other hand are unmistakable. But if, though unjust, I acquire the reputation of justice, a heavenly life is promised to me.

"Since then, as philosophers prove, appearance tyrannizes over truth and is lord of happiness, to appearance I must devote myself. I will describe around me a picture and shadow of virtue to be the vestibule and exterior of my house; behind I will trail the subtle and crafty fox, as Archilochus, greatest of sages, recommends."
refashioned:
"The youth reasons: 'Can I reach a higher tower -- one that protects me all my days -- through justice, or through the crooked paths of deceit?'

"Because everything he hears tells him: if I am truly just but not recognized as just, there is no benefit whatsoever -- only obvious pain and loss. But if I am unjust while maintaining a reputation for justice, I am promised paradise.

"Since appearances rule over truth and determine happiness -- as the philosophers themselves demonstrate -- I must dedicate myself to appearances. I will paint a portrait of virtue as the front entrance to my life, a beautiful facade. But behind it, I will be the cunning fox that Archilochus, wisest of poets, recommends."
elegant: The rational young person concludes: since being just without appearing just brings only suffering, while appearing just without being just brings every reward, appearances must be mastered. Virtue will serve as a painted facade; behind it, one should cultivate the cunning of a fox, as the poet Archilochus advised.
summary: Adeimantus voices the rational conclusion of a bright young person: since justice without reputation brings only pain, while injustice with a virtuous appearance brings every reward, the logical strategy is to build a facade of virtue while practicing cunning deception behind it.
distilled: The rational strategy is to construct a facade of virtue while practicing the cunning of a fox behind it, since appearance governs happiness, not reality.
notes: Adeimantus speaking (in the voice of the rational youth) -- Pindar: the 'loftier tower' quotation is from a lost work -- Archilochus: Greek lyric poet of Paros (c. 680-645 BCE), known for his fable of the fox and the hedgehog -- 'The subtle and crafty fox': from Archilochus's fable, the fox represents cunning adaptability -- 'Appearance tyrannizes over truth': this phrase encapsulates the challenge Socrates must overcome -- The 'vestibule and exterior' metaphor: a painted facade of virtue concealing inner vice -- Cross-reference: Republic Book II (382a-383c) on the 'true lie' versus the 'lie in words' -- Cross-reference: Machiavelli, The Prince ch. 18, on the necessity of appearing virtuous while acting like a fox -- This is the climax of the rational case for injustice

narrative:
"'But I hear some one exclaiming that the concealment of wickedness is often difficult;' to which I answer: 'Nothing great is easy.' Nevertheless, the argument indicates this, if we would be happy, to be the path along which we should proceed.

"With a view to concealment we will establish secret brotherhoods and political clubs. And there are professors of rhetoric who teach the art of persuading courts and assemblies; and so, partly by persuasion and partly by force, I shall make unlawful gains and not be punished.

"'Still I hear a voice saying that the gods cannot be deceived, neither can they be compelled.'"
refashioned:
"Someone objects: 'But it is often difficult to hide wickedness.' My answer: nothing great comes easily. Still, the argument points clearly in this direction -- this is the path to happiness.

"To manage concealment, we will form secret societies and political networks. There are teachers of rhetoric who train people in the art of persuading courts and assemblies. So through a combination of persuasion and force, I will make illicit profits without facing punishment.

"But then another voice objects: 'The gods cannot be fooled, and they cannot be forced.'"
elegant: The objection that concealment is difficult is dismissed: nothing great is easy. Secret brotherhoods, political networks, and rhetorical training will handle human detection. The remaining concern is divine oversight -- can the gods be deceived or compelled?
summary: Adeimantus addresses the practical objection that hiding wickedness is hard: secret societies, political connections, and rhetorical skill can manage human detection. The only remaining obstacle is the gods -- can they be fooled or overridden?
distilled: Concealing injustice from humans is manageable through secret alliances and rhetoric; the only remaining question is whether the gods can be deceived.
notes: Adeimantus speaking (continuing the youth's reasoning) -- 'Secret brotherhoods and political clubs' (hetairiai and synomosiai): these were real features of Athenian politics, associated with oligarchic conspiracies like the Thirty Tyrants (404-403 BCE) -- 'Professors of rhetoric': the Sophists, whom Plato consistently criticizes -- The argument's structure: (1) concealment is difficult but manageable, (2) human institutions can be manipulated, (3) divine oversight remains as the final obstacle -- Cross-reference: Republic Book I (337d-338c), the Sophists' role in corrupting justice -- Cross-reference: Gorgias 452d-453a on rhetoric as the art of persuasion

narrative:
"But what if there are no gods? Or, suppose them to have no care of human things -- why in either case should we mind about concealment?

"And even if there are gods, and they do care about us, yet we know of them only from tradition and the genealogies of the poets; and these are the very persons who say that they may be influenced and turned by 'sacrifices and soothing entreaties and by offerings.' Let us be consistent then, and believe both or neither.

"If the poets speak truly, why then we had better be unjust, and offer of the fruits of injustice; for if we are just, although we may escape the vengeance of heaven, we shall lose the gains of injustice; but, if we are unjust, we shall keep the gains, and by our sinning and praying, and praying and sinning, the gods will be propitiated, and we shall not be punished.

"'But there is a world below in which either we or our posterity will suffer for our unjust deeds.' Yes, my friend, will be the reflection, but there are mysteries and atoning deities, and these have great power. That is what mighty cities declare; and the children of the gods, who were their poets and prophets, bear a like testimony."
refashioned:
"But what if there are no gods at all? Or what if gods exist but do not concern themselves with human affairs? In either case, why worry about being found out?

"And even if gods do exist and do care about us, our only knowledge of them comes from tradition and the poets' genealogies -- and these same poets say the gods can be swayed by sacrifices and prayers and offerings. We should be consistent: believe all of what the poets say, or none of it.

"If the poets are right, the rational move is to be unjust and offer the gods a share of the profits. If we are just, we might avoid divine punishment -- but we lose all the gains of injustice. If we are unjust, we keep the gains, and through a cycle of sinning and praying, praying and sinning, we can appease the gods and escape punishment.

"'But what about the afterlife? Won't we or our descendants suffer for our crimes?' Perhaps -- but there are mystery rites and atoning deities with great power. Mighty cities officially endorse these rites, and the children of the gods -- their poets and prophets -- testify to their effectiveness."
elegant: The final obstacle -- divine punishment -- collapses under scrutiny. If there are no gods, or if they are indifferent, concealment is unnecessary. If gods exist and do care, the poets (our only source of knowledge about them) say they can be bribed. Consistency demands we accept this too. The rational strategy: be unjust, share the profits with the gods through sacrifice, and buy off any afterlife consequences through mystery rites that powerful cities officially endorse.
summary: Adeimantus demolishes the last defense of justice -- divine punishment. If gods do not exist or do not care, there is nothing to fear. If they do exist and care, the same poetic tradition that tells us about them says they can be bribed with sacrifices. Even afterlife punishments can be averted through mystery rites endorsed by major cities.
distilled: Divine punishment poses no barrier to injustice: if gods exist, the poets say they can be bribed; if the afterlife threatens, mystery rites promise atonement.
notes: Adeimantus speaking (continuing the youth's reasoning) -- 'What if there are no gods': one of the earliest articulations of the atheistic challenge in Western philosophy -- 'Sinning and praying, and praying and sinning': a devastating summary of instrumental religion -- 'Mighty cities': Athens, Eleusis, and other cities that officially sponsored mystery rites -- 'Children of the gods': Orpheus, Musaeus, and other legendary poets with claimed divine parentage -- The argument is a reductio: if our only knowledge of gods comes from poets, and poets say gods can be bribed, then religion provides no check on injustice -- Cross-reference: Laws Book X (885b-907d), Plato's most systematic treatment of atheism and theology -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (614b-621d), where the Myth of Er provides Socrates' counter-account of divine justice

narrative:
"On what principle, then, shall we any longer choose justice rather than the worst injustice? When, if we only unite the latter with a deceitful regard to appearances, we shall fare to our mind both with gods and men, in life and after death, as the most numerous and the highest authorities tell us.

"Knowing all this, Socrates, how can a man who has any superiority of mind or person or rank or wealth, be willing to honour justice; or indeed to refrain from laughing when he hears justice praised?

"And even if there should be some one who is able to disprove the truth of my words, and who is satisfied that justice is best, still he is not angry with the unjust, but is very ready to forgive them, because he also knows that men are not just of their own free will; unless, peradventure, there be some one whom the divinity within him may have inspired with a hatred of injustice, or who has attained knowledge of the truth -- but no other man.

"He only blames injustice who, owing to cowardice or age or some weakness, has not the power of being unjust. And this is proved by the fact that when he obtains the power, he immediately becomes unjust as far as he can be."
refashioned:
"On what grounds, then, should anyone still choose justice over the worst injustice? If we combine injustice with a convincing appearance of virtue, we will prosper with both gods and men, in this life and the next -- or so the most numerous and most respected authorities tell us.

"Knowing all this, Socrates, how can any person of intelligence, strength, status, or wealth take justice seriously -- or even keep from laughing when they hear it praised?

"And even someone who could refute everything I have said and genuinely believes justice is best -- even that person would not be angry at the unjust. He would readily forgive them, knowing that no one is just by choice. The only exceptions might be someone divinely inspired with a hatred of injustice, or someone who has arrived at genuine knowledge of the truth -- but no one else.

"The people who condemn injustice are always those too cowardly, too old, or too weak to be unjust themselves. The proof? The moment they gain the power, they become as unjust as they possibly can."
elegant: Why choose justice when injustice combined with a virtuous facade guarantees success with gods and men alike? Any person of ability would laugh at justice being praised. Even those who believe justice is best forgive the unjust, knowing no one chooses justice freely. Only divine inspiration or true knowledge could make someone genuinely just. Everyone who condemns injustice does so from weakness -- and proves it by becoming unjust the moment they gain power.
summary: Adeimantus concludes his argument: given everything that has been said, any rational person of ability would choose injustice over justice. Those who condemn injustice do so only from weakness, as proven by the fact that they become unjust as soon as they gain the power to do so.
distilled: No rational person of ability would choose justice; those who condemn injustice do so only because they lack the power to be unjust.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- 'The divinity within him': the daimonion, Socrates' famous inner voice (cf. Apology 31c-d, Phaedrus 242b-c) -- 'Attained knowledge of the truth': hints that philosophical knowledge might be the only genuine basis for justice -- This echoes the Ring of Gyges conclusion: people are just only from inability to be unjust -- The claim that the weak condemn injustice because they cannot practice it is a form of Nietzsche's later 'slave morality' thesis (Genealogy of Morals) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (580d-583a), where Socrates argues the philosopher's pleasure is greatest -- Cross-reference: Gorgias 483a-484c, Callicles' argument that conventional justice is the revenge of the weak

narrative:
"The cause of all this, Socrates, was indicated by us at the beginning of the argument, when my brother and I told you how astonished we were to find that of all the professing panegyrists of justice -- beginning with the ancient heroes of whom any memorial has been preserved to us, and ending with the men of our own time -- no one has ever blamed injustice or praised justice except with a view to the glories, honours, and benefits which flow from them.

"No one has ever adequately described either in verse or prose the true essential nature of either of them abiding in the soul, and invisible to any human or divine eye; or shown that of all the things of a man's soul which he has within him, justice is the greatest good, and injustice the greatest evil.

"Had this been the universal strain, had you sought to persuade us of this from our youth upwards, we should not have been on the watch to keep one another from doing wrong, but every one would have been his own watchman, because afraid, if he did wrong, of harbouring in himself the greatest of evils.

"I dare say that Thrasymachus and others would seriously hold the language which I have been merely repeating, and words even stronger than these about justice and injustice, grossly, as I conceive, perverting their true nature."
refashioned:
"The root cause of all this, Socrates, is what my brother and I pointed out at the start: we were astonished to discover that throughout all of history -- from the ancient heroes whose records survive to the thinkers of our own time -- no one has ever praised justice or condemned injustice except by pointing to external rewards and punishments.

"No one has ever adequately described, in poetry or prose, the true essential nature of justice and injustice as they exist within the soul, invisible to any human or divine observer. No one has shown that justice is the greatest good a soul can possess, and injustice the greatest evil.

"If this had been the universal teaching -- if we had been taught from childhood that justice is the soul's greatest treasure -- we would not need to police each other's behavior. Every person would be their own guardian, terrified of harboring the greatest of evils within themselves.

"I suspect Thrasymachus and others genuinely believe the arguments I have been presenting as devil's advocate -- and would put them even more forcefully. They are, I believe, deeply distorting the true nature of justice and injustice."
elegant: The fundamental problem: throughout all of history, no one has praised justice for what it actually is within the soul. Every defense has relied on external rewards. If people had been taught from youth that justice is the soul's greatest good and injustice its greatest evil, external enforcement would be unnecessary -- everyone would guard their own soul. This absence of genuine moral education is what allows arguments like Thrasymachus's to thrive.
summary: Adeimantus identifies the core failure: no one in all of history has defended justice as an intrinsic good of the soul rather than by pointing to external rewards. If people understood that justice is the soul's greatest good, they would police themselves rather than needing laws to constrain them.
distilled: The root problem is that no one has ever shown justice to be the soul's greatest intrinsic good; every defense has relied on external rewards and punishments.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- 'No one has ever adequately described': this is the precise gap the Republic aims to fill -- 'Every one would have been his own watchman': anticipates Kant's notion of moral autonomy and the internal moral law -- 'Invisible to any human or divine eye': justice in the soul is not directly observable, hence the challenge -- 'Thrasymachus and others': Adeimantus again distances himself from the immoralist position -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (427d-445e), where Socrates finally defines justice in the soul as the proper ordering of its three parts -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (588b-592b), the image of the soul as a composite creature -- The passage sets the entire agenda for Books II-IX

narrative:
"But I speak in this vehement manner, as I must frankly confess to you, because I want to hear from you the opposite side; and I would ask you to show not only the superiority which justice has over injustice, but what effect they have on the possessor of them which makes the one to be a good and the other an evil to him.

"And please, as Glaucon requested of you, to exclude reputations; for unless you take away from each of them his true reputation and add on the false, we shall say that you do not praise justice, but the appearance of it; we shall think that you are only exhorting us to keep injustice dark, and that you really agree with Thrasymachus in thinking that justice is another's good and the interest of the stronger, and that injustice is a man's own profit and interest, though injurious to the weaker."
refashioned:
"I am arguing this passionately, I freely admit, because I want to hear you make the opposite case. Show us not just that justice is superior to injustice, but what each of them does to the person who possesses it -- what makes one a genuine good and the other a genuine evil for the possessor.

"And as Glaucon requested: strip away all reputations. Unless you remove the true reputation from each and replace it with a false one -- making the just man appear unjust and vice versa -- we will conclude that you are praising not justice itself, but merely the appearance of justice.
