We will think you are simply advising us to hide our injustice better, and that you secretly agree with Thrasymachus: that justice is really just a benefit to others and a tool of the powerful, while injustice is what actually serves the individual's own interest."
elegant: Adeimantus demands that Socrates show what justice and injustice do to the soul of the person who possesses them. All reputations must be excluded: if Socrates defends justice by its external rewards, he is merely praising appearance, not justice itself, and secretly conceding Thrasymachus's point that justice serves others' interests, not one's own.
summary: Adeimantus specifies the challenge precisely: Socrates must show what justice and injustice do to the soul of the possessor, with all reputations stripped away. Any defense relying on external rewards would amount to agreeing with Thrasymachus that justice is merely another's good.
distilled: Show what justice does to the soul of the person who has it, stripped of all reputation -- or concede that justice merely serves others' interests.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- Restates and sharpens Glaucon's challenge from 2.3 -- 'Another's good and the interest of the stronger': Thrasymachus's definition from Republic Book I (338c) -- The method of excluding reputation is the key methodological constraint of the entire inquiry -- 'Keep injustice dark': if Socrates only argues for appearing just, he is actually teaching concealed injustice -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (443c-444a), where Socrates defines justice as the soul's internal harmony -- Cross-reference: Republic Book I (343b-344c), Thrasymachus's full argument

narrative:
"Now as you have admitted that justice is one of that highest class of goods which are desired indeed for their results, but in a far greater degree for their own sakes -- like sight or hearing or knowledge or health, or any other real and natural and not merely conventional good -- I would ask you in your praise of justice to regard one point only: I mean the essential good and evil which justice and injustice work in the possessors of them.

"Let others praise justice and censure injustice, magnifying the rewards and honours of the one and abusing the other; that is a manner of arguing which, coming from them, I am ready to tolerate, but from you who have spent your whole life in the consideration of this question, unless I hear the contrary from your own lips, I expect something better.

"And therefore, I say, not only prove to us that justice is better than injustice, but show what they either of them do to the possessor of them, which makes the one to be a good and the other an evil, whether seen or unseen by gods and men."
refashioned:
"You have already admitted that justice belongs to the highest class of goods -- those desired for their results but far more for their own sakes, like sight, hearing, knowledge, or health. These are real, natural goods, not merely conventional ones. I ask you to focus on one thing only: the essential good and evil that justice and injustice produce in the people who possess them.

"Let other people praise justice and condemn injustice by pointing to rewards and honors -- I can tolerate that from ordinary thinkers. But from you, Socrates, who have devoted your entire life to this question, I expect more. Unless you tell me otherwise, I hold you to a higher standard.

"So prove to us not only that justice is better than injustice, but show what each of them does to its possessor that makes one a good and the other an evil -- whether observed or unobserved by gods and men."
elegant: Since Socrates has placed justice among the highest goods -- valued intrinsically like sight, health, and knowledge -- Adeimantus demands he defend it accordingly. Focus on one thing only: what justice and injustice do to the soul of the possessor, visible or invisible to gods and men. From someone who has spent his whole life on this question, nothing less will do.
summary: Adeimantus holds Socrates to his own standard: since justice supposedly belongs to the highest class of intrinsic goods, Socrates must show what it does within the possessor's soul -- regardless of whether gods or men can observe it -- and not rely on external rewards like lesser thinkers.
distilled: Prove that justice is an intrinsic good of the soul -- not by citing rewards, but by showing what it does to the person who possesses it, seen or unseen.
notes: Adeimantus speaking -- This is the final, definitive formulation of the challenge that drives the entire Republic -- 'Sight or hearing or knowledge or health': the paradigm cases of intrinsic goods from Glaucon's taxonomy in 2.2 -- 'Whether seen or unseen by gods and men': the most demanding possible standard -- justice must be good even if no one (not even the gods) ever knows about it -- 'Spent your whole life in the consideration of this question': acknowledges Socrates' philosophical vocation -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (443c-e), where the answer begins to emerge: justice is internal harmony of the soul -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (580d-588a), where the three proofs of justice's superiority are given

narrative:
I had always admired the genius of Glaucon and Adeimantus, but on hearing these words I was quite delighted, and said:

"Sons of an illustrious father, that was not a bad beginning of the Elegiac verses which the admirer of Glaucon made in honour of you after you had distinguished yourselves at the battle of Megara:

'Sons of Ariston,' he sang, 'divine offspring of an illustrious hero.'

The epithet is very appropriate, for there is something truly divine in being able to argue as you have done for the superiority of injustice, and remaining unconvinced by your own arguments. And I do believe that you are not convinced -- this I infer from your general character, for had I judged only from your speeches I should have mistrusted you.

"But now, the greater my confidence in you, the greater is my difficulty in knowing what to say. For I am in a strait between two; on the one hand I feel that I am unequal to the task; and my inability is brought home to me by the fact that you were not satisfied with the answer which I made to Thrasymachus, proving, as I thought, the superiority which justice has over injustice. And yet I cannot refuse to help, while breath and speech remain to me; I am afraid that there would be an impiety in being present when justice is evil spoken of and not lifting up a hand in her defence. And therefore I had best give such help as I can."
refashioned:
I had always admired the brilliance of Glaucon and Adeimantus, but hearing these words I was genuinely delighted. I said:

"Sons of a distinguished father -- that elegiac verse someone composed in your honor after you fought at the battle of Megara was well put: 'Sons of Ariston, divine offspring of an illustrious hero.' The description fits perfectly, because there is something truly remarkable about being able to argue so powerfully for injustice's superiority and yet remain unconvinced by your own arguments. I believe you genuinely are unconvinced -- I can tell from your character, because if I judged you only by these speeches, I would be suspicious.

"But the more I trust your sincerity, the harder it is for me to know what to say. I am caught between two difficulties: I feel unequal to the task -- especially since you were not satisfied with my earlier argument against Thrasymachus. And yet I cannot stand by silently. As long as I have breath and the power of speech, it would be impious to hear justice attacked and not lift a hand in its defense. So I must do the best I can."
elegant: Socrates praises Glaucon and Adeimantus for their remarkable ability to argue powerfully for injustice while remaining personally unconvinced. He confesses he feels unequal to the challenge, especially since his earlier argument against Thrasymachus proved insufficient. Yet he cannot remain silent when justice is attacked -- it would be impious. He must do his best.
summary: Socrates responds to the brothers' challenge with admiration for their intellectual honesty and a confession of inadequacy. Though he feels overmatched, he declares it would be impious to stand by while justice is maligned, and resolves to defend it as best he can.
distilled: Socrates feels unequal to the challenge but declares it would be impious to remain silent while justice is attacked.
notes: Socrates speaking to Adeimantus (and Glaucon) -- 'Sons of Ariston': Ariston was the father of Plato, Glaucon, and Adeimantus -- The battle of Megara: a historical event in which the brothers distinguished themselves militarily -- 'Ariston' means 'best' in Greek, making 'divine offspring of the best' a double meaning -- Socrates' confession of inadequacy is characteristically Socratic (cf. the Apology's claim of knowing only that he knows nothing) -- 'Impiety' in not defending justice: Socrates treats the defense of justice as a religious obligation -- Cross-reference: Apology 28d-29a, where Socrates says he cannot abandon his philosophical post -- The transition from challenge to response: Socrates now takes up the defense

narrative:
Glaucon and the rest entreated me by all means not to let the question drop, but to proceed in the investigation. They wanted to arrive at the truth, first, about the nature of justice and injustice, and secondly, about their relative advantages.

I told them, what I really thought, that the enquiry would be of a serious nature, and would require very good eyes.

"Seeing then," I said, "that we are no great wits, I think that we had better adopt a method which I may illustrate thus: suppose that a short-sighted person had been asked by some one to read small letters from a distance; and it occurred to some one else that they might be found in another place which was larger and in which the letters were larger -- if they were the same and he could read the larger letters first, and then proceed to the lesser -- this would have been thought a rare piece of good fortune."
refashioned:
Glaucon and the others urged me not to abandon the inquiry but to press forward. They wanted to discover the truth about the nature of justice and injustice, and about which is truly more advantageous.

I told them honestly that this was a deeply serious investigation requiring sharp vision.

"Since we are not the sharpest minds," I said, "let me suggest a method by analogy. Imagine a nearsighted person was asked to read small letters from a distance. Then someone pointed out that the same letters also appeared elsewhere, written much larger. If they could read the larger version first and then turn to the smaller, that would be a tremendous stroke of luck."
elegant: Socrates proposes a method for the investigation: since justice in the individual soul is hard to discern, like reading small letters from a distance, they should first examine justice on a larger scale where it will be easier to see, and then apply the findings to the smaller case.
summary: Socrates introduces the analogy of reading small letters versus large ones: since justice is difficult to examine in the individual soul, they should first study it in something larger where it will be more visible, then apply those findings to the individual.
distilled: To understand justice in the individual, first examine it at a larger scale where it is easier to see -- reading the large letters before the small.
notes: Socrates speaking -- The 'large letters' analogy is the methodological foundation for the city-soul analogy that structures the entire Republic -- This introduces the principle of structural isomorphism: justice in the state mirrors justice in the individual -- 'We are no great wits': characteristic Socratic modesty/irony -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (434d-435c), where Socrates explicitly transfers the definition of justice from state to individual -- The analogy assumes that justice has the same structure at both scales -- a key (and contested) premise of the Republic

narrative:
"Very true," said Adeimantus; "but how does the illustration apply to our enquiry?"

"I will tell you," I replied. "Justice, which is the subject of our enquiry, is, as you know, sometimes spoken of as the virtue of an individual, and sometimes as the virtue of a State."

"True," he replied.

"And is not a State larger than an individual?"

"It is."

"Then in the larger the quantity of justice is likely to be larger and more easily discernible. I propose therefore that we enquire into the nature of justice and injustice, first as they appear in the State, and secondly in the individual, proceeding from the greater to the lesser and comparing them."

"That," he said, "is an excellent proposal."

"And if we imagine the State in process of creation, we shall see the justice and injustice of the State in process of creation also."

"I dare say."

"When the State is completed there may be a hope that the object of our search will be more easily discovered."

"Yes, far more easily."

"But ought we to attempt to construct one?" I said; "for to do so, as I am inclined to think, will be a very serious task. Reflect therefore."

"I have reflected," said Adeimantus, "and am anxious that you should proceed."

"A State," I said, "arises, as I conceive, out of the needs of mankind; no one is self-sufficing, but all of us have many wants. Can any other origin of a State be imagined?"

"There can be no other."

"Then, as we have many wants, and many persons are needed to supply them, one takes a helper for one purpose and another for another; and when these partners and helpers are gathered together in one habitation the body of inhabitants is termed a State."

"True," he said.

"And they exchange with one another, and one gives, and another receives, under the idea that the exchange will be for their good."

"Very true."

"Then," I said, "let us begin and create in idea a State; and yet the true creator is necessity, who is the mother of our invention."
refashioned:
"True enough," said Adeimantus, "but how does this analogy apply to our inquiry?"

"Justice," I said, "is sometimes spoken of as the virtue of an individual and sometimes as the virtue of a state."

"Correct."

"And a state is larger than an individual?"

"Obviously."

"Then justice in the larger entity should be easier to see. Let us examine justice and injustice as they appear in the state first, and then in the individual -- moving from the greater to the lesser and comparing them."

"An excellent approach," he said.

"And if we imagine a state being founded from scratch, we should be able to watch justice and injustice come into being along with it."

"Quite likely."

"Once the state is complete, we will have a better chance of finding what we are looking for."

"Much better."

"Shall we attempt it, then? It will be a serious undertaking."

"I have thought about it," said Adeimantus, "and I am eager for you to proceed."

"A state arises," I said, "from human needs. No one is self-sufficient -- we all have many wants. Can you imagine any other origin for a state?"

"None."

"Since we have many needs and many people are required to meet them, we come together as partners and helpers, each contributing something different. When these partners gather in one place, the resulting community is called a state."

"Right."

"And they trade with each other, each giving and receiving, because they believe exchange benefits them."

"Exactly."

"Then let us construct a state in our imagination. Though really, the true architect is necessity -- the mother of invention."
elegant: Socrates proposes examining justice in the state first, since it is larger and therefore easier to discern, then applying the findings to the individual. He begins constructing an ideal state from first principles: human beings are not self-sufficient, so they form communities to meet mutual needs through specialization and exchange. Necessity is the mother of invention -- and of the state.
summary: Socrates proposes studying justice in the state before the individual, since the larger scale makes it easier to see. He begins building an ideal state from its foundation: human neediness and mutual dependence. No one is self-sufficient, so people gather together, specialize, and trade -- forming a state out of necessity.
distilled: To find justice, first build a state from scratch: people form communities because no one is self-sufficient, and necessity drives specialization and exchange.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The city-soul analogy is the structural backbone of the Republic -- 'Necessity, the mother of invention': a proverbial expression Socrates adapts -- The state arises from need (chreia), not from a social contract (contrast with Glaucon's account in 2.4) -- Socrates' account is functionalist: the state exists to meet human needs through cooperation -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (434d-445e), where the structural analogy between state and soul is made explicit -- Cross-reference: Politics 1252a-1253a, where Aristotle develops his own account of the state's origin -- The 'state in process of creation' method is a genetic analysis: understanding a thing by watching it come into being

narrative:
"Of course," he replied.

"Now the first and greatest of necessities is food, which is the condition of life and existence."

"Certainly."

"The second is a dwelling, and the third clothing and the like."

"True."

"And now let us see how our city will be able to supply this great demand: We may suppose that one man is a husbandman, another a builder, some one else a weaver -- shall we add to them a shoemaker, or perhaps some other purveyor to our bodily wants?"

"Quite right."

"The barest notion of a State must include four or five men."
refashioned:
"Of course," he replied.

"The first and most fundamental necessity is food -- the condition of life itself."

"Certainly."

"The second is shelter, and the third is clothing and similar needs."

"True."

"Now, how will our city meet this demand? We can start with one person as a farmer, another as a builder, another as a weaver -- and perhaps add a shoemaker or someone else to supply basic bodily needs."

"That sounds right."

"So the most minimal conceivable state requires four or five people."
elegant: The most basic human needs are food, shelter, and clothing. Meeting these requires at minimum a farmer, a builder, a weaver, and a shoemaker -- four or five people. This is the skeleton of a state.
summary: Socrates identifies the three most basic human needs -- food, shelter, and clothing -- and determines that the smallest possible state requires four or five specialized workers: a farmer, builder, weaver, and shoemaker.
distilled: The minimal state is four or five people specializing to meet the basic needs of food, shelter, and clothing.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The hierarchy of needs (food, shelter, clothing) anticipates Maslow's hierarchy of needs by millennia -- The minimal state of 4-5 people establishes the principle of specialization that will become central to Plato's theory of justice -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (433a-b), where justice is defined as each person doing their own proper work -- The material basis of the state: Plato starts from physical necessities, not from ideas or ideals

narrative: "Clearly. And how will they proceed? Will each bring the result of his labours into a common stock? -- the individual husbandman, for example, producing for four, and labouring four times as long and as much as he need in the provision of food with which he supplies others as well as himself; or will he have nothing to do with others and not be at the trouble of producing for them, but provide for himself alone a fourth of the food in a fourth of the time, and in the remaining three-fourths of his time be employed in making a house or a coat or a pair of shoes, having no partnership with others, but supplying himself all his own wants?"
refashioned: "Now, how will these people organize their work? Will each contribute their output to a common pool? For instance, will the farmer produce enough food for all four people, working four times as long as he would need for himself alone? Or will each person work independently -- the farmer spending only a quarter of his time on food for himself, then using the remaining three-quarters to build his own house, make his own clothing, and cobble his own shoes, with no cooperation or exchange?"
elegant: The fundamental economic question: should each person specialize and share their output with the group, or should each person try to meet all their own needs independently? Should the farmer produce for four, or only for himself?
summary: Socrates poses the foundational question of economic organization: should workers specialize and contribute to a common pool, or should each person independently try to meet all their own needs?
distilled: The key question: is it better for each person to specialize and share, or for everyone to try to be self-sufficient?
notes: Socrates speaking -- This passage frames the fundamental choice between specialization/trade and autarky/self-sufficiency -- The answer (given in 2.30) establishes the principle of the division of labor -- Cross-reference: Adam Smith, Wealth of Nations Book I, ch. 1-3 on the division of labor -- The 'common stock' option anticipates both socialist and market economies -- Plato's answer will favor specialization, which becomes the basis of his definition of justice

narrative:
Adeimantus thought that he should aim at producing food only and not at producing everything.

"Probably," I replied, "that would be the better way; and when I hear you say this, I am myself reminded that we are not all alike; there are diversities of natures among us which are adapted to different occupations."

"Very true."

"And will you have a work better done when the workman has many occupations, or when he has only one?"

"When he has only one."

"Further, there can be no doubt that a work is spoilt when not done at the right time?"

"No doubt."

"For business is not disposed to wait until the doer of the business is at leisure; but the doer must follow up what he is doing, and make the business his first object."

"He must."

"And if so, we must infer that all things are produced more plentifully and easily and of a better quality when one man does one thing which is natural to him and does it at the right time, and leaves other things."

"Undoubtedly."

"Then more than four citizens will be required; for the husbandman will not make his own plough or mattock, or other implements of agriculture, if they are to be good for anything. Neither will the builder make his tools -- and he too needs many; and in like manner the weaver and shoemaker."

"True."

"Then carpenters, and smiths, and many other artisans, will be sharers in our little State, which is already beginning to grow?"

"True."

"Yet even if we add neatherds, shepherds, and other herdsmen, in order that our husbandmen may have oxen to plough with, and builders as well as husbandmen may have draught cattle, and curriers and weavers fleeces and hides -- still our State will not be very large."

"That is true; yet neither will it be a very small State which contains all these."

"Then, again, there is the situation of the city -- to find a place where nothing need be imported is well-nigh impossible."

"Impossible."

"Then there must be another class of citizens who will bring the required supply from another city?"
refashioned:
Adeimantus agreed that the farmer should focus on producing food rather than trying to produce everything.

"That is probably the better approach," I said. "And your answer reminds me of something important: we are not all the same. Different people have different natural aptitudes suited to different kinds of work."

"True."

"Is work done better when a person has many jobs or just one?"

"Just one."

"And work suffers when it is not done at the right time?"

"Certainly."

"Because the work will not wait for the worker to get around to it -- the worker must attend to it as a priority."

"He must."

"So everything is produced in greater quantity, more easily, and at higher quality when each person does the one thing that suits their nature, does it at the right time, and leaves everything else to others."

"Without question."

"Then we will need more than four citizens. The farmer cannot make his own plow and tools if they are to be any good. Neither can the builder make his own tools -- and he needs many. Same for the weaver and shoemaker."

"True."

"So carpenters, blacksmiths, and many other craftspeople will join our growing little state."

"Yes."

"And if we add cattle herders, shepherds, and other herdsmen -- so farmers can have oxen for plowing, builders and farmers can have draft animals, and tanners and weavers can have hides and fleeces -- the state is still not very large."

"Not very large, but not small either, once it includes all these."

"And consider the city's location: it is nearly impossible to find a place where nothing needs to be imported."

"Impossible."

"Then we need another class of citizens -- merchants who bring needed supplies from other cities."
elegant: Specialization is superior: people have different natural aptitudes, work is done better when each person focuses on one task, and timing matters. This principle of the division of labor means the state must grow beyond four citizens to include toolmakers, herders, and eventually merchants for imported goods -- since no city can be entirely self-sufficient.
summary: Socrates establishes the principle of specialization: work is done better, faster, and in greater quantity when each person focuses on one task suited to their nature. This principle drives the state's growth from four basic workers to include toolmakers, herders, and merchants for trade with other cities.
distilled: The division of labor produces better results: each person should do one thing suited to their nature, which requires an expanding community including craftspeople, herders, and merchants.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'Diversities of natures adapted to different occupations': the principle of natural aptitude that will ground Plato's entire theory of justice -- 'One man does one thing which is natural to him': this is the formula for justice in Republic Book IV (433a): 'doing one's own work' (to ta hautou prattein) -- The expanding state: farmer > builder > weaver > shoemaker > toolmakers > herders > merchants -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (433a-434c), the definition of justice as each doing their proper work -- Cross-reference: Adam Smith's pin factory example (Wealth of Nations I.1) -- The recognition that no city is self-sufficient introduces the necessity of trade and international relations

narrative:
"There must. But if the trader goes empty-handed, having nothing which they require who would supply his need, he will come back empty-handed."

"That is certain."

"And therefore what they produce at home must be not only enough for themselves, but such both in quantity and quality as to accommodate those from whom their wants are supplied."

"Very true."

"Then more husbandmen and more artisans will be required?"

"They will."

"Not to mention the importers and exporters, who are called merchants?"

"Yes."

"Then we shall want merchants?"

"We shall."

"And if merchandise is to be carried over the sea, skilful sailors will also be needed, and in considerable numbers?"

"Yes, in considerable numbers."

"Then, again, within the city, how will they exchange their productions? To secure such an exchange was, as you will remember, one of our principal objects when we formed them into a society and constituted a State."

"Clearly they will buy and sell."

"Then they will need a market-place, and a money-token for purposes of exchange."

"Certainly."

"Suppose now that a husbandman, or an artisan, brings some production to market, and he comes at a time when there is no one to exchange with him -- is he to leave his calling and sit idle in the market-place?"

"Not at all; he will find people there who, seeing the want, undertake the office of salesmen. In well-ordered States they are commonly those who are the weakest in bodily strength, and therefore of little use for any other purpose; their duty is to be in the market, and to give money in exchange for goods to those who desire to sell and to take money from those who desire to buy."

"This want, then, creates a class of retail-traders in our State. Is not 'retailer' the term which is applied to those who sit in the market-place engaged in buying and selling, while those who wander from one city to another are called merchants?"

"Yes," he said.

"And there is another class of servants, who are intellectually hardly on the level of companionship; still they have plenty of bodily strength for labour, which accordingly they sell, and are called, if I do not mistake, hirelings, hire being the name which is given to the price of their labour."
refashioned:
"There must be merchants. But a trader who arrives with nothing his trading partners need will return empty-handed."

"Obviously."

"So domestic production must exceed local needs -- both in quantity and quality -- to have something worth trading for imports."

"True."

"Which means we need more farmers and more craftspeople."

"Yes."

"Plus importers and exporters -- the people called merchants."

"Right."

"And if goods travel by sea, we will need skilled sailors in considerable numbers."

"Considerable numbers, yes."

"Now, within the city itself, how do people exchange what they produce? Facilitating exchange was, after all, one of our main reasons for forming a community."

"They will buy and sell."

"So they need a marketplace and some form of currency."

"Certainly."

"But what if a farmer or craftsman brings goods to market and no buyer is there? Should he abandon his work and wait around?"

"No -- there will be people who see the need and take on the role of middlemen. In well-run states, these tend to be people who are physically weak and not suited for other work. They stay in the marketplace, buying goods from sellers for money and selling them to buyers."

"So this need creates a class of retail traders. 'Retailers' are those who buy and sell in the marketplace, while 'merchants' are those who trade between cities."

"Correct."

"There is also another class: workers who may not be intellectually equipped for other roles but have strong bodies. They sell their physical labor, and we call them wage-laborers -- 'hire' being the term for the price of their work."
elegant: Trade requires surplus production. The state needs merchants for inter-city trade, sailors for sea commerce, a marketplace and currency for internal exchange, and retail traders to facilitate transactions. Physical laborers who sell their bodily strength for wages round out the economy. Each role arises from a specific need that the principle of specialization demands be filled by dedicated workers.
summary: Socrates traces how trade necessitates surplus production, merchants, sailors, a marketplace, currency, retail traders as middlemen, and wage-laborers -- each role arising from practical necessity and filled by those best suited to it.
distilled: Trade drives the emergence of merchants, sailors, marketplaces, currency, retailers, and wage-laborers -- each role filled by specialized workers suited to the task.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The distinction between merchants (emporoi, inter-city traders) and retailers (kapeloi, marketplace middlemen) reflects actual Greek economic categories -- 'Weakest in bodily strength': Plato's view that retail trade suits those unfit for physical labor -- 'Hirelings' (misthotoi): wage-laborers who sell physical strength -- The emergence of money (nomisma) as a 'token for exchange' -- Cross-reference: Aristotle, Politics 1256b-1258a, on natural vs. unnatural forms of exchange -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (421d-422a) on the guardian class not handling money -- The economic structure is growing: producers > craftspeople > herders > merchants > sailors > retailers > wage-laborers

narrative:
"True. Then hirelings will help to make up our population?"

"Yes."

"And now, Adeimantus, is our State matured and perfected?"

"I think so."

"Where, then, is justice, and where is injustice, and in what part of the State did they spring up?"

"Probably in the dealings of these citizens with one another. I cannot imagine that they are more likely to be found anywhere else."

"I dare say that you are right in your suggestion," I said; "we had better think the matter out, and not shrink from the enquiry."
refashioned:
"So wage-laborers complete our population."

"Yes."

"Then, Adeimantus -- is our state now complete?"

"I think so."

"So where is justice in this state? Where is injustice? At what point did they arise?"

"Probably in the interactions between citizens. I cannot imagine they would be found anywhere else."

"You may well be right," I said. "Let us think it through carefully and not shy away from the investigation."
elegant: With the basic state complete, Socrates asks the central question: where do justice and injustice arise? Adeimantus suggests they emerge in the dealings between citizens. Socrates agrees this deserves careful investigation.
summary: With the minimal state now constructed, Socrates poses the key question: where do justice and injustice originate? Adeimantus suggests they arise in citizens' interactions with each other.
distilled: With the basic state built, the central question surfaces: where do justice and injustice arise in the interactions between citizens?
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'Is our State matured and perfected?': this is the 'city of necessity' or 'healthy city' (later called the 'city of pigs' by Glaucon) -- The question 'where is justice?' will not be fully answered until Book IV (432b-434d) -- Adeimantus's suggestion that justice lies in citizens' dealings prefigures the eventual definition: justice is each person performing their proper function -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (433a-b): justice is each class doing its own work and not meddling with others -- The 'healthy city' represents a baseline that will be contrasted with the 'luxurious city' or 'feverish city' that follows

narrative:
"Let us then consider, first of all, what will be their way of life, now that we have thus established them. Will they not produce corn, and wine, and clothes, and shoes, and build houses for themselves? And when they are housed, they will work, in summer, commonly, stripped and barefoot, but in winter substantially clothed and shod.

"They will feed on barley-meal and flour of wheat, baking and kneading them, making noble cakes and loaves; these they will serve up on a mat of reeds or on clean leaves, themselves reclining the while upon beds strewn with yew or myrtle. And they and their children will feast, drinking of the wine which they have made, wearing garlands on their heads, and hymning the praises of the gods, in happy converse with one another.

"And they will take care that their families do not exceed their means; having an eye to poverty or war."
refashioned:
"Now let us consider how these citizens will actually live. They will grow grain, make wine, produce clothing and shoes, and build houses. In summer they will work lightly dressed and barefoot; in winter, warmly clothed and shod.

"For food, they will bake barley and wheat into good bread and cakes, serving them on mats of reeds or clean leaves. They will recline on beds of yew or myrtle branches. They and their children will feast together, drinking their own wine, wearing garlands, singing hymns to the gods, and enjoying each other's company.

"They will be careful not to let their families grow beyond what they can support, keeping watch against both poverty and war."
elegant: The citizens of the healthy city live simply: growing food, making clothes, building houses. They feast on bread and wine, recline on natural beds, sing hymns, and enjoy fellowship. They practice moderation, keeping their families within sustainable limits to avoid poverty and war.
summary: Socrates describes the daily life of the healthy city: simple agriculture, modest feasting on bread and wine, communal celebration with hymns to the gods, and careful family planning to maintain sustainability and avoid war.
distilled: Citizens of the healthy city live simply, feast modestly, celebrate together, and limit their families to avoid poverty and war.
notes: Socrates speaking -- This is Socrates' vision of the 'true and healthy constitution' of the state -- a pastoral ideal -- The description echoes Hesiod's Works and Days and the Golden Age mythology -- 'Having an eye to poverty or war': population control as civic prudence -- 'Noble cakes and loaves': even in simplicity, there is dignity -- The garlands and hymns suggest a life of natural piety -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (398e-399e), where simple music and lifestyle are prescribed for the guardians -- Glaucon will immediately mock this as the 'city of pigs' (2.35) -- The tension between Socrates' healthy city and Glaucon's demand for luxury drives the remainder of Book II

narrative:
But Glaucon interposed: "You have not given them a relish to their meal."

"True," I replied, "I had forgotten; of course they must have a relish -- salt, and olives, and cheese, and they will boil roots and herbs such as country people prepare; for a dessert we shall give them figs, and peas, and beans; and they will roast myrtle-berries and acorns at the fire, drinking in moderation. And with such a diet they may be expected to live in peace and health to a good old age, and bequeath a similar life to their children after them."
refashioned:
Glaucon cut in: "You have not given them anything to put on their bread."

"You are right," I said, "I forgot. They will need salt, olives, and cheese. They will cook roots and herbs in the country style. For dessert: figs, chickpeas, and beans. They will roast myrtle-berries and acorns by the fire, drinking wine in moderation. On this diet, they can expect to live in peace and good health to a ripe old age, passing the same way of life on to their children."
elegant: Socrates adds simple condiments and country fare to the diet -- salt, olives, cheese, herbs, figs, beans, roasted acorns -- and promises that this moderate lifestyle will bring peace, health, and longevity passed down through generations.
summary: When Glaucon notes the meal lacks condiments, Socrates adds simple country fare -- salt, olives, cheese, herbs, figs, and beans -- and promises that this moderate diet will bring peace, health, and long life across generations.
distilled: A simple, moderate diet of country fare brings peace, health, and longevity -- the natural reward of the healthy city.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- 'Relish' (opson): anything eaten with bread; the basic Greek diet was bread plus accompaniments -- The foods listed are stereotypical peasant fare -- Myrtle-berries and acorns: wild foraged foods, emphasizing the pastoral simplicity -- 'Drinking in moderation': sophrosyne (temperance/moderation) is one of the cardinal virtues explored in the Republic -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (404a-c) on the simple diet prescribed for guardians -- This description sets up Glaucon's sarcastic rejoinder about the 'city of pigs'

narrative:
"Yes, Socrates," Glaucon said, "and if you were providing for a city of pigs, how else would you feed the beasts?"

"But what would you have, Glaucon?" I replied.

"Why," he said, "you should give them the ordinary conveniences of life. People who are to be comfortable are accustomed to lie on sofas, and dine off tables, and they should have sauces and sweets in the modern style."
refashioned:
"Socrates," Glaucon said, "if you were designing a city of pigs, you would feed them exactly the same way."

"What would you prefer?" I asked.

"Give them the ordinary comforts of civilized life," he said. "People who are going to live well expect to recline on couches, eat at proper tables, and have sauces and desserts in the current fashion."
elegant: Glaucon dismisses Socrates' healthy city as a "city of pigs" and demands civilized comforts: couches, tables, sauces, and sweets. Human beings, he insists, require more than bare necessities.
summary: Glaucon mocks Socrates' simple city as fit only for pigs and demands that its citizens be given civilized comforts -- couches, tables, fine food, and modern amenities.
distilled: Glaucon ridicules the healthy city as a 'city of pigs' and demands the comforts of civilized luxury.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- 'City of pigs' (he ton huon polis): one of the Republic's most famous phrases -- Glaucon's objection is that Socrates' city meets animal needs but not distinctly human ones -- The demand for luxury will drive the transition from the 'healthy city' to the 'feverish city' -- 'Modern style': Glaucon wants contemporary Athenian refinements -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IX (588b-e), where the soul is compared to a composite creature including a many-headed beast -- The philosophical tension: are luxury and culture essential to human flourishing, or sources of corruption?

narrative:
"Yes," I said, "now I understand: the question which you would have me consider is, not only how a State, but how a luxurious State is created; and possibly there is no harm in this, for in such a State we shall be more likely to see how justice and injustice originate.

"In my opinion the true and healthy constitution of the State is the one which I have described. But if you wish also to see a State at fever heat, I have no objection. For I suspect that many will not be satisfied with the simpler way of life. They will be for adding sofas, and tables, and other furniture; also dainties, and perfumes, and incense, and courtesans, and cakes, all these not of one sort only, but in every variety; we must go beyond the necessaries of which I was at first speaking, such as houses, and clothes, and shoes: the arts of the painter and the embroiderer will have to be set in motion, and gold and ivory and all sorts of materials must be procured."
refashioned:
"I see," I said. "The question you want me to consider is not just how a state is formed, but how a luxurious state comes into being. There may be no harm in this -- in fact, a luxurious state might show us more clearly where justice and injustice originate.

"For the record, I believe the true and healthy constitution is the simple one I just described. But if you want to see a state in a state of fever, I have no objection. Many people will not be content with simplicity. They will want couches, tables, fine furniture, delicacies, perfumes, incense, courtesans, elaborate pastries -- and not just one variety of each, but every kind imaginable. We will have to go far beyond the basic necessities of houses, clothing, and shoes. The arts of painting and embroidery will need to be developed. Gold, ivory, and all sorts of luxury materials will need to be acquired."
elegant: Socrates concedes: examining a luxurious state may better reveal where justice and injustice originate. He considers his simple city the true, healthy constitution, while the luxurious state is a city in fever. Luxury demands an explosion of wants beyond necessity -- fine furniture, delicacies, perfumes, courtesans, decorative arts, gold, and ivory.
summary: Socrates agrees to examine a luxurious state, which may better reveal the origins of justice and injustice. He regards his simple city as the truly healthy constitution and the luxurious version as a city 'at fever heat,' driven by desires that multiply far beyond necessity.
distilled: The simple city is healthy; the luxurious city is feverish -- but examining luxury may better reveal where justice and injustice originate.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- 'True and healthy constitution' vs. 'State at fever heat': a medical metaphor -- the simple city is healthy, the luxurious one diseased -- The list of luxuries (perfumes, incense, courtesans, cakes) represents the appetitive element of the soul writ large -- 'Courtesans' (hetairai): the inclusion signals moral corruption -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (427d), where the completed luxurious city is where justice will be found -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VIII (555b-562a), where the progression of corrupt regimes follows from excessive desire -- The transition from healthy to feverish city mirrors the soul's potential corruption by appetite

narrative:
"True," he said.

"Then we must enlarge our borders; for the original healthy State is no longer sufficient. Now will the city have to fill and swell with a multitude of callings which are not required by any natural want; such as the whole tribe of hunters and actors, of whom one large class have to do with forms and colours; another will be the votaries of music -- poets and their attendant train of rhapsodists, players, dancers, contractors; also makers of divers kinds of articles, including women's dresses.

"And we shall want more servants. Will not tutors be also in request, and nurses wet and dry, tirewomen and barbers, as well as confectioners and cooks; and swineherds, too, who were not needed and therefore had no place in the former edition of our State, but are needed now? They must not be forgotten: and there will be animals of many other kinds, if people eat them."
refashioned:
"True," Glaucon said.

"Then we must expand our borders, because the original healthy state is no longer sufficient. The city will swell with occupations that serve no natural necessity: hunters, actors -- some working with visual forms and colors -- and the devotees of music: poets and their retinue of reciters, performers, dancers, and event organizers. Add manufacturers of every kind of luxury item, including fine clothing.

"We will also need more servants: tutors, wet-nurses and nannies, hairdressers and barbers, pastry chefs and professional cooks. And swineherds -- who had no place in the earlier, simpler state but are now required. We must not forget them. And there will be livestock of many kinds, if people are going to eat meat."
elegant: The luxurious city must expand to accommodate occupations serving no natural need: hunters, actors, artists, poets, musicians, dancers, manufacturers of luxury goods, tutors, servants, barbers, chefs, and swineherds. The simple state's borders cannot contain the proliferation of desires.
summary: The luxurious city swells with occupations that serve no natural necessity -- actors, artists, poets, musicians, luxury manufacturers, personal servants, and professional cooks. The original healthy state's borders can no longer contain the expanding population driven by multiplying desires.
distilled: Luxury demands an explosion of non-essential occupations -- artists, entertainers, servants, and cooks -- swelling the city far beyond its healthy original size.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- 'Not required by any natural want': the key distinction between necessary and unnecessary desires, developed fully in Republic Book VIII (558d-559d) -- 'Swineherds': pigs were not needed in the simple city (an ironic callback to Glaucon's 'city of pigs' remark) -- 'Rhapsodists': professional reciters of epic poetry, like Ion in Plato's dialogue of that name -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (394e-398b) on restricting imitative arts -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-608b), the famous critique and expulsion of poets -- The catalog of luxury occupations illustrates how appetite, unchecked, generates endless social complexity

narrative:
"Certainly. And living in this way we shall have much greater need of physicians than before?"

"Much greater."

"And the country which was enough to support the original inhabitants will be too small now, and not enough?"

"Quite true."

"Then a slice of our neighbours' land will be wanted by us for pasture and tillage, and they will want a slice of ours, if, like ourselves, they exceed the limit of necessity, and give themselves up to the unlimited accumulation of wealth?"

"That, Socrates, will be inevitable."

"And so we shall go to war, Glaucon. Shall we not?"

"Most certainly," he replied.

"Then without determining as yet whether war does good or harm, thus much we may affirm, that now we have discovered war to be derived from causes which are also the causes of almost all the evils in States, private as well as public."

"Undoubtedly."

"And our State must once more enlarge; and this time the enlargement will be nothing short of a whole army, which will have to go out and fight with the invaders for all that we have, as well as for the things and persons whom we were describing above."

"Why?" he said; "are they not capable of defending themselves?"

"No," I said; "not if we were right in the principle which was acknowledged by all of us when we were framing the State: the principle, as you will remember, was that one man cannot practise many arts with success."
refashioned:
"And this luxurious lifestyle will create a much greater need for doctors."

"Much greater."

"And the territory that supported the original population will no longer be sufficient."

"Correct."

"So we will need a portion of our neighbors' land for grazing and farming -- and they will want a portion of ours, if they too have exceeded the limits of necessity and given themselves over to unlimited wealth accumulation."

"That is inevitable, Socrates."

"And so we will go to war, Glaucon."

"Most certainly."

"Without yet deciding whether war is beneficial or harmful, we can at least say this: we have discovered that war arises from the same causes that produce nearly all the evils in states, both public and private."

"Without question."

"So our state must expand again -- this time by adding an entire army to defend everything we have and everyone we have described."

"But why can't the citizens defend themselves?" Glaucon asked.

"Because of the principle we already established: no one can practice many skills successfully."
elegant: Luxury breeds disease (requiring more doctors), territorial expansion (requiring neighbors' land), and inevitably war. War, Socrates observes, springs from the same unlimited desire that causes nearly all evils in states. The principle of specialization then demands a professional army -- citizens cannot double as soldiers any more than farmers can double as shoemakers.
summary: Luxury leads to disease, territorial expansion, and war -- which Socrates identifies as arising from the same unlimited desires that cause nearly all evils in states. The principle of specialization then requires a dedicated professional army, since citizens cannot effectively serve as part-time soldiers.
distilled: Luxury breeds war through unlimited desire for territory and wealth, and the principle of specialization demands a professional warrior class.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- 'Unlimited accumulation of wealth' (apeiros ploutou ktesis): identified as the root cause of war and most political evil -- 'War derived from causes which are also the causes of almost all evils': a remarkable claim linking imperialism to domestic corruption -- The principle of specialization now extends to warfare: this introduces the guardian class -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-417b), the education of the guardians -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (419a-421c), the guardians' way of life -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VIII (547b-569c), where the corruption of the state follows from desire for wealth -- The logical chain: luxury > territorial need > war > professional army > guardian class

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"But is not war an art?"

"Certainly."

"And an art requiring as much attention as shoemaking?"

"Quite true."

"And the shoemaker was not allowed by us to be a husbandman, or a weaver, or a builder -- in order that we might have our shoes well made; but to him and to every other worker was assigned one work for which he was by nature fitted, and at that he was to continue working all his life long and at no other; he was not to let opportunities slip, and then he would become a good workman.

"Now nothing can be more important than that the work of a soldier should be well done. But is war an art so easily acquired that a man may be a warrior who is also a husbandman, or shoemaker, or other artisan; although no one in the world would be a good dice or draught player who merely took up the game as a recreation, and had not from his earliest years devoted himself to this and nothing else?"
refashioned:
"True," he said.

"But warfare is a skill, is it not?"

"Certainly."

"And one that demands as much dedication as shoemaking?"

"Absolutely."

"We did not allow our shoemaker to also be a farmer, weaver, or builder -- we wanted well-made shoes. Every worker was assigned the one task suited to their nature and expected to practice it for life, never letting opportunities pass, so they could become truly excellent.

"Now, nothing is more important than warfare being done well. But is war so simple an art that a person can be a soldier while also being a farmer, shoemaker, or some other tradesman? No one would expect to become a good chess or backgammon player by treating the game as a hobby. You have to devote yourself to it from the very beginning."
elegant: If shoemaking requires lifelong specialization, warfare -- which is far more important -- demands it even more. A part-time soldier is as absurd as a part-time chess master. The principle of specialization that governs every other craft applies with even greater force to the art of war.
summary: Socrates argues that if shoemaking requires dedicated specialization, warfare demands it even more. Just as no one becomes an expert at board games through casual play, no one can be a competent soldier while also practicing another trade.
distilled: If every craft requires lifelong specialization, warfare -- the most important craft -- demands it most of all.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon in dialogue -- The argument from specialization is now applied to create the guardian (phylax) class -- 'Dice or draught player': board games (pessoi, petteia) used as analogies for skill requiring dedicated practice -- The comparison of warfare to shoemaking is deliberately provocative: it elevates craft-knowledge while challenging aristocratic assumptions about natural military prowess -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (374d-376c), where the qualities of the guardian nature are described -- Cross-reference: Laches 181a-184c, where the value of military training is discussed -- This argument is the birth of the professional military class in political philosophy

narrative: "No tools will make a man a skilled workman, or master of defence, nor be of any use to him who has not learned how to handle them, and has never bestowed any attention upon them. How then will he who takes up a shield or other implement of war become a good fighter all in a day, whether with heavy-armed or any other kind of troops?"
refashioned: "No set of tools will make someone a skilled craftsman or a capable defender if they have never learned to use them and have never practiced. How, then, can someone pick up a shield or any other weapon of war and become a competent soldier overnight -- whether as a heavy infantryman or in any other role?"
elegant: Tools alone do not create skill. Without training and practice, picking up a shield does not make a soldier -- no more than picking up a hammer makes a carpenter. Professional warfare requires professional preparation.
summary: Socrates argues that tools alone cannot create competence: without dedicated training, picking up a shield no more makes someone a soldier than picking up tools makes someone a craftsman.
distilled: Tools without training are useless -- you cannot become a soldier by simply picking up a shield.
notes: Socrates speaking -- This short passage reinforces the specialization argument for a dedicated military class -- 'Heavy-armed' (hoplites): the standard Greek citizen-soldier; Socrates is arguing against the Athenian practice of citizen-militia warfare -- The argument challenges Athenian democratic military practice, where citizens were expected to serve as soldiers -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (374d-e), guardian selection criteria -- Cross-reference: Laches, which is entirely devoted to the question of what courage is and how it relates to military expertise -- This completes the logical case for a professional guardian class distinct from the productive class

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "the tools which would teach men their own use would be beyond price."

"And the higher the duties of the guardian," I said, "the more time, and skill, and art, and application will be needed by him?"

"No doubt," he replied.

"Will he not also require natural aptitude for his calling?"

"Certainly."

"Then it will be our duty to select, if we can, natures which are fitted for the task of guarding the city?"

"It will."

"And the selection will be no easy matter," I said; "but we must be brave and do our best."

"We must."

"Is not the noble youth very like a well-bred dog in respect of guarding and watching?"

"What do you mean?"

"I mean that both of them ought to be quick to see, and swift to overtake the enemy when they see him; and strong too if, when they have caught him, they have to fight with him."

"All these qualities," he replied, "will certainly be required by them."

"Well, and your guardian must be brave if he is to fight well?"

"Certainly."

"And is he likely to be brave who has no spirit, whether horse or dog or any other animal? Have you never observed how invincible and unconquerable is spirit and how the presence of it makes the soul of any creature to be absolutely fearless and indomitable?"

"I have."

"Then now we have a clear notion of the bodily qualities which are required in the guardian."

"True."

"And also of the mental ones; his soul is to be full of spirit?"

"Yes."

"But are not these spirited natures apt to be savage with one another, and with everybody else?"

"A difficulty by no means easy to overcome," he replied.

"Whereas," I said, "they ought to be dangerous to their enemies, and gentle to their friends; if not, they will destroy themselves without waiting for their enemies to destroy them."

"True," he said.

"What is to be done then?" I said; "how shall we find a gentle nature which has also a great spirit, for the one is the contradiction of the other?"
refashioned:
"Yes," Glaucon said, "tools that could teach their own use would be priceless."

"And the greater the guardian's responsibilities," I said, "the more time, skill, craft, and dedication they require."

"No doubt."

"So guardians must also have a natural aptitude for the role?"

"Of course."

"Then we must select -- if we can -- those natures fitted for guarding the city."

"We must."

"This will not be easy, but we have to try our best."

"Agreed."

"Think of it this way: isn't a noble young guardian very much like a well-bred dog?"

"How so?"

"Both need sharp perception to spot the enemy, speed to pursue, and strength to fight when they catch up."

"Those qualities are certainly necessary."

"And the guardian must be courageous to fight well?"

"Obviously."

"But can anyone be brave who lacks spirit -- whether horse, dog, or any other creature? Have you noticed that spirit makes the soul of any living thing absolutely fearless and unconquerable?"

"I have."

"So we now know the physical requirements: perception, speed, strength. And the mental requirement: a spirited soul."

"Yes."

"But here is the problem: spirited natures tend to be aggressive -- savage toward everyone, not just enemies."

"A serious difficulty," he admitted.

"Yet guardians must be fierce toward enemies and gentle toward friends. Otherwise they will destroy each other before the enemy has a chance to."

"True."

"So how do we find a nature that combines gentleness with great spirit? The two seem to contradict each other."
elegant: The guardian needs natural aptitude: sharp perception, speed, strength, and above all, spirit -- the quality that makes any creature fearless. But spirited natures tend toward savagery. Guardians must be fierce to enemies yet gentle to friends, or they will destroy themselves. The apparent contradiction: how can one nature combine gentleness with great spirit?

Guardians must be fierce toward enemies yet gentle toward friends, creating an apparent contradiction that must be resolved.

Republic Book I, 332d) -- The paradox of combining gentleness and spiritedness prepares for the introduction of the philosophical nature -- Cross-reference: Republic Book IV (439e-441c), the tripartite soul: reason, spirit, appetite -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (375a-376c), continuation of guardian nature discussion

narrative:
"True. He will not be a good guardian who is wanting in either of these two qualities; and yet the combination of them appears to be impossible; and hence we must infer that to be a good guardian is impossible."

"I am afraid that what you say is true," he replied.

Here feeling perplexed I began to think over what had preceded.

"My friend," I said, "no wonder that we are in a perplexity; for we have lost sight of the image which we had before us."

"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean to say that there do exist natures gifted with those opposite qualities."

"And where do you find them?"

"Many animals," I replied, "furnish examples of them; our friend the dog is a very good one: you know that well-bred dogs are perfectly gentle to their familiars and acquaintances, and the reverse to strangers."

"Yes, I know."

"Then there is nothing impossible or out of the order of nature in our finding a guardian who has a similar combination of qualities?"

"Certainly not."

"Would not he who is fitted to be a guardian, besides the spirited nature, need to have the qualities of a philosopher?"

"I do not apprehend your meaning."

"The trait of which I am speaking," I replied, "may be also seen in the dog, and is remarkable in the animal."

"What trait?"

"Why, a dog, whenever he sees a stranger, is angry; when an acquaintance, he welcomes him, although the one has never done him any harm, nor the other any good. Did this never strike you as curious?"

"The matter never struck me before; but I quite recognise the truth of your remark."

"And surely this instinct of the dog is very charming -- your dog is a true philosopher."

"Why?"

"Why, because he distinguishes the face of a friend and of an enemy only by the criterion of knowing and not knowing. And must not an animal be a lover of learning who determines what he likes and dislikes by the test of knowledge and ignorance?"

"Most assuredly."

"And is not the love of learning the love of wisdom, which is philosophy?"
refashioned:
"A guardian lacking either quality -- gentleness or spirit -- will not serve. But combining them seems impossible, which would make a good guardian impossible."

"I am afraid you are right," Glaucon said.

Feeling stuck, I reconsidered the problem.

"Wait," I said. "We have lost sight of something obvious. There are natures that combine these opposite qualities."

"Where?"

"In many animals. Take the well-bred dog: perfectly gentle with people it knows, and hostile to strangers."

"True enough."

"So there is nothing unnatural about finding a guardian with the same combination."

"Agreed."

"But the ideal guardian needs something beyond spirit. They need to be, in a sense, a philosopher."

"What do you mean?"

"Look at the dog again. There is a remarkable trait: a dog is hostile to every stranger and friendly to every acquaintance -- regardless of whether the stranger has ever harmed it or the acquaintance ever helped it. Have you noticed this?"

"Now that you mention it, yes."

"This makes the dog a natural philosopher."

"How?"

"Because the dog distinguishes friend from enemy purely by the criterion of knowing versus not-knowing. Any creature that sorts the world based on knowledge and ignorance must be called a lover of learning. And what is the love of learning if not the love of wisdom -- philosophy?"
elegant: The paradox is resolved by the example of the well-bred dog, which is naturally gentle with those it knows and fierce with strangers. The dog distinguishes friend from foe not by who has helped or harmed it, but by the criterion of knowledge versus ignorance. This makes the dog, in a sense, a philosopher -- a lover of learning. The ideal guardian must likewise combine spirit with a philosophical nature.
summary: Socrates resolves the guardian paradox with the analogy of the dog: well-bred dogs are gentle to those they know and hostile to strangers, distinguishing friend from foe by knowledge alone. Since the dog sorts the world by knowing versus not-knowing, it is a natural philosopher -- and the guardian must similarly combine spiritedness with a love of wisdom.

"That we may safely affirm."

"Then he who is to be a really good and noble guardian of the State will require to unite in himself philosophy and spirit and swiftness and strength?"

"Undoubtedly."

"Then we have found the desired natures; and now that we have found them, how are they to be reared and educated? Is not this enquiry which may be expected to throw light on the greater enquiry which is our final end -- How do justice and injustice grow up in States? For we do not want either to omit what is to the point or to draw out the argument to an inconvenient length."

narrative:
Adeimantus thought that the enquiry would be of great service to us.

"Then," I said, "my dear friend, the task must not be given up, even if somewhat long."

"Certainly not."

"Come then, and let us pass a leisure hour in story-telling, and our story shall be the education of our heroes."

"By all means."

"And what shall be their education? Can we find a better than the traditional sort? -- and this has two divisions, gymnastic for the body, and music for the soul."

"True."

"Shall we begin education with music, and go on to gymnastic afterwards?"

"By all means."

"And when you speak of music, do you include literature or not?"

"I do."

"And literature may be either true or false?"

"Yes."

"And the young should be trained in both kinds, and we begin with the false?"

"I do not understand your meaning," he said.

"You know," I said, "that we begin by telling children stories which, though not wholly destitute of truth, are in the main fictitious; and these stories are told them when they are not of an age to learn gymnastics."

"Very true."

"That was my meaning when I said that we must teach music before gymnastics."

"Quite right," he said.

"You know also that the beginning is the most important part of any work, especially in the case of a young and tender thing; for that is the time at which the character is being formed and the desired impression is more readily taken."

"Quite true."

"And shall we just carelessly allow children to hear any casual tales which may be devised by casual persons, and to receive into their minds ideas for the most part the very opposite of those which we should wish them to have when they are grown up?"

"We cannot."

"Then the first thing will be to establish a censorship of the writers of fiction, and let the censors receive any tale of fiction which is good, and reject the bad; and we will desire mothers and nurses to tell their children the authorised ones only. Let them fashion the mind with such tales, even more fondly than they mould the body with their hands; but most of those which are now in use must be discarded."
refashioned:
Adeimantus agreed this inquiry would be very useful.

"Then we must not abandon it, even if it takes time," I said.

"Absolutely not."

"Let us spend some time telling a story -- and the story will be the education of our guardians."

"Let us."

"What should their education look like? The traditional system has two parts: physical training for the body, and music for the soul. Can we improve on this?"

"That seems right."

"Should we start with music and move to physical training later?"

"Yes."

"When I say 'music,' I include literature. And literature can be either true or fictional."

"Right."

"We train the young in both kinds, beginning with fiction."

"What do you mean?" Adeimantus asked.

"We start teaching children with stories -- stories that are mostly fictional, though they contain elements of truth. These stories are told at an age when children are too young for physical training."

"That is true."

"This is why we must begin with music before gymnastics."

"Makes sense."

"And you know that the beginning of any work is the most important part -- especially with something young and impressionable. Early childhood is when character is formed and impressions are most easily stamped."

"Absolutely."

"So should we carelessly let children hear any random stories invented by random people, absorbing ideas that are often the exact opposite of what we want them to believe as adults?"

"We cannot."

"Then the first step is to establish oversight of fiction writers. Censors will approve good stories and reject bad ones. Mothers and nurses should tell children only the approved stories. Let them shape the mind with stories even more carefully than they shape the body with their hands. Most of the stories currently in use must be thrown out."
elegant: Education begins with music (including literature), since stories shape character in early childhood when impressions are most lasting. Children must not be exposed to random tales that instill wrong ideas. Fiction writers must be supervised: good stories approved, bad ones rejected. Stories shape the mind even more powerfully than hands shape the body, and most current stories must be discarded.
summary: Socrates begins designing guardian education with the traditional two-part curriculum: music (including literature) for the soul and gymnastics for the body, starting with music. Since early childhood stories powerfully shape character, fiction must be censored -- only approved stories should be told to children, and most existing tales must be discarded.

narrative:
"Of what tales are you speaking?" he said.

"You may find a model of the lesser in the greater," I said; "for they are necessarily of the same type, and there is the same spirit in both of them."

"Very likely," he replied; "but I do not as yet know what you would term the greater."

"Those," I said, "which are narrated by Homer and Hesiod, and the rest of the poets, who have ever been the great story-tellers of mankind."

"But which stories do you mean," he said; "and what fault do you find with them?"

"A fault which is most serious," I said; "the fault of telling a lie, and, what is more, a bad lie."

"But when is this fault committed?"

"Whenever an erroneous representation is made of the nature of gods and heroes -- as when a painter paints a portrait not having the shadow of a likeness to the original."

"Yes," he said, "that sort of thing is certainly very blamable; but what are the stories which you mean?"
refashioned:
"Which tales are you referring to?" Adeimantus asked.

"The smaller stories follow the pattern of the larger ones -- they share the same character and spirit."

"Probably so, but which are the larger stories?"

"The ones told by Homer, Hesiod, and the other great poets -- the master storytellers of the human race."

"What stories specifically, and what is wrong with them?"

"The most serious fault possible: they tell lies -- and bad lies at that."

"When does this happen?"

"Whenever they misrepresent the nature of gods and heroes. It is like a painter creating a portrait that bears no resemblance whatsoever to the original subject."

"That is certainly blameworthy. But which stories do you mean specifically?"

summary: Socrates identifies the problematic stories as those of Homer, Hesiod, and the other major poets, whose most serious fault is misrepresenting the nature of gods and heroes -- telling lies that bear no resemblance to the truth, like a portrait nothing like its subject.
distilled: Homer and Hesiod commit the gravest fault: lying about the nature of gods and heroes through false representations.

narrative:
"First of all," I said, "there was that greatest of all lies, in high places, which the poet told about Uranus, and which was a bad lie too -- I mean what Hesiod says that Uranus did, and how Cronus retaliated on him. The doings of Cronus, and the sufferings which in turn his son inflicted upon him, even if they were true, ought certainly not to be lightly told to young and thoughtless persons; if possible, they had better be buried in silence.

"But if there is an absolute necessity for their mention, a chosen few might hear them in a mystery, and they should sacrifice not a common [Eleusinian] pig, but some huge and unprocurable victim; and then the number of the hearers will be very few indeed."
refashioned:
"The greatest and worst of these lies," I said, "is the story Hesiod tells about Uranus -- what Uranus did and how Cronus retaliated against him. And then what Cronus's son Zeus did to him in turn. Even if these stories were true, they should never be casually told to young, impressionable minds. Ideally, they should be buried in total silence.

"If they absolutely must be mentioned, they should be restricted to a select few, told within the secrecy of a mystery rite -- and the price of admission should not be the common Eleusinian pig, but some enormous and nearly impossible-to-obtain sacrificial animal. That way the audience would be vanishingly small."
elegant: The worst theological lies are Hesiod's stories of divine succession: Uranus's crimes, Cronus's retaliation, Zeus's overthrow of Cronus. Even if true, such stories of divine violence should be suppressed entirely or restricted to the tiniest possible audience through prohibitively expensive mystery rites.

narrative:
"Neither, if we mean our future guardians to regard the habit of quarrelling among themselves as of all things the basest, should any word be said to them of the wars in heaven, and of the plots and fightings of the gods against one another, for they are not true.

"No, we shall never mention the battles of the giants, or let them be embroidered on garments; and we shall be silent about the innumerable other quarrels of gods and heroes with their friends and relatives.

"If they would only believe us we would tell them that quarrelling is unholy, and that never up to this time has there been any quarrel between citizens; this is what old men and old women should begin by telling children; and when they grow up, the poets also should be told to compose for them in a similar spirit.

"But the narrative of Hephaestus binding Hera his mother, or how on another occasion Zeus sent him flying for taking her part when she was being beaten, and all the battles of the gods in Homer -- these tales must not be admitted into our State, whether they are supposed to have an allegorical meaning or not.

"For a young person cannot judge what is allegorical and what is literal; anything that he receives into his mind at that age is likely to become indelible and unalterable; and therefore it is most important that the tales which the young first hear should be models of virtuous thoughts."
refashioned:
"If we want our guardians to consider internal conflict the most shameful thing possible, they must never hear about wars among the gods or plots of divine beings against each other -- because these stories are not true.

"We will never mention the battles of the giants. They will not appear embroidered on clothing. We will say nothing about the countless quarrels between gods and heroes and their own friends and family.

"Ideally, we would teach children from the very start that quarreling is unholy, and that citizens have never fought among themselves. This is what the elderly should tell children first, and what poets should compose as children grow up.

"The story of Hephaestus binding his mother Hera, Zeus hurling Hephaestus from heaven for defending her, and all the divine battles in Homer -- none of these can be admitted into our state. It does not matter whether they are meant allegorically.

"A young person cannot tell allegory from literal truth. Whatever enters the mind at that age tends to become permanent and unalterable. Therefore the first stories children hear must be models of virtuous thinking."

The first stories children hear must therefore be models of virtue.
