narrative:
"There you are right," he replied; "but if any one asks where are such models to be found and of what tales are you speaking -- how shall we answer him?"

I said to him: "You and I, Adeimantus, at this moment are not poets, but founders of a State: now the founders of a State ought to know the general forms in which poets should cast their tales, and the limits which must be observed by them, but to make the tales is not their business."

"Very true," he said; "but what are these forms of theology which you mean?"

"Something of this kind," I replied: "God is always to be represented as he truly is, whatever be the sort of poetry, epic, lyric or tragic, in which the representation is given."

"Right."

"And is he not truly good? And must he not be represented as such?"

"Certainly."

"And no good thing is hurtful?"

"No, indeed."

"And that which is not hurtful hurts not?"

"Certainly not."

"And that which hurts not does no evil?"

"No."

"And can that which does no evil be a cause of evil?"

"Impossible."

"And the good is advantageous?"

"Yes."

"And therefore the cause of well-being?"

"Yes."

"It follows therefore that the good is not the cause of all things, but of the good only?"

"Assuredly."

"Then God, if he be good, is not the author of all things, as the many assert, but he is the cause of a few things only, and not of most things that occur to men. For few are the goods of human life, and many are the evils, and the good is to be attributed to God alone; of the evils the causes are to be sought elsewhere, and not in him."
refashioned:
"You are right," Adeimantus said. "But if someone asks where these model stories can be found, what do we say?"

"You and I are not poets, Adeimantus -- we are founders of a state. Founders need to know the general principles that poets must follow and the boundaries they must respect. Actually writing the stories is not our job."

"Fair enough. But what are these theological principles?"

"This: God must always be represented as he truly is, in every form of poetry -- epic, lyric, or tragic."

"Agreed."

"And God is truly good? And must be represented as such?"

"Of course."

"Now: nothing that is good is harmful."

"Correct."

"What is not harmful does not cause harm."

"Right."

"What causes no harm does no evil."

"True."

"And what does no evil cannot be a cause of evil."

"Impossible."

"The good is beneficial -- the cause of well-being."

"Yes."

"Therefore the good is not the cause of everything, but only of good things."

"Necessarily."

"Then God, being good, is not the author of all things -- as most people believe. God is the cause of only a few things that happen to human beings: the good things. The evil things, which are far more numerous, must have causes elsewhere. They cannot come from God."
elegant: Founders of a state set the theological principles that poets must follow. The first principle: God must be represented as truly good. Through a chain of reasoning -- good is not harmful, what is not harmful does no evil, what does no evil cannot cause evil -- Socrates concludes that God is not the author of all things, but only of the good. Evil, which far outnumbers good in human life, must have other causes.
summary: Socrates establishes the first principle of theology for the ideal state: God is good and must be represented as such. Since the good cannot cause evil, God is the author only of good things. The many evils of human life must have causes other than God.
distilled: First theological principle: God is good and therefore the cause of good only -- evil must come from other sources.

narrative:
"Zeus, who is the dispenser of good and evil to us."

"And if any one asserts that the violation of oaths and treaties, which was really the work of Pandarus, was brought about by Athene and Zeus, or that the strife and contention of the gods was instigated by Themis and Zeus, he shall not have our approval; neither will we allow our young men to hear the words of Aeschylus, that

'God plants guilt among men when he desires utterly to destroy a house.'

"And if a poet writes of the sufferings of Niobe -- the subject of the tragedy in which these iambic verses occur -- or of the house of Pelops, or of the Trojan war or on any similar theme, either we must not permit him to say that these are the works of God, or if they are of God, he must devise some explanation of them such as we are seeking; he must say that God did what was just and right, and they were the better for being punished; but that those who are punished are miserable, and that God is the author of their misery -- the poet is not to be permitted to say; though he may say that the wicked are miserable because they require to be punished, and are benefited by receiving punishment from God; but that God being good is the author of evil to any one is to be strenuously denied, and not to be said or sung or heard in verse or prose by any one whether old or young in any well-ordered commonwealth. Such a fiction is suicidal, ruinous, impious."
refashioned:
"Nor can we accept the idea of Zeus as the dispenser of both good and evil.

"If anyone claims that Athena and Zeus caused Pandarus to break oaths and treaties -- when it was really Pandarus's own doing -- or that Zeus and Themis instigated strife among the gods, we will not approve. We will not let young people hear Aeschylus say that 'God plants guilt in men when he wishes to utterly destroy a house.'

"If a poet writes about the sufferings of Niobe, the house of Pelops, the Trojan War, or any similar theme, he must not say these calamities are God's doing. Or if he does attribute them to God, he must explain that God acted justly and that the punished were improved by their punishment. But the poet must never say that the punished are miserable and that God caused their misery. He may say the wicked suffer because they need punishment and benefit from it. But the claim that God, being good, is the author of evil to anyone must be vigorously denied. It must never be spoken, sung, or heard -- in verse or prose, by young or old -- in any well-governed state. Such a claim is self-destructive, ruinous, and impious."
elegant: Poets must not attribute human wrongdoing to divine instigation, as Homer does with Pandarus, or claim God plants guilt in men to destroy them, as Aeschylus does. When depicting suffering, poets may say the wicked benefit from divine punishment, but never that God is the author of misery. The claim that God causes evil must be absolutely prohibited in any well-governed state -- it is suicidal, ruinous, and impious.
summary: Socrates prohibits attributing human wrongdoing to divine causation and bars poets from saying God is the author of evil or misery. Punishment may be depicted as beneficial to the punished, but God causing evil must be denied absolutely in every form of expression throughout the state.
distilled: Poets must never claim God causes evil or plants guilt in men -- such a fiction is self-destructive and impious.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Pandarus: a Trojan who broke the truce (Iliad 4.86-126); Homer has Athena prompt him, but Socrates insists the responsibility was Pandarus's own -- Aeschylus, fragment from a lost play: 'God plants guilt (ate) among men when he wishes to destroy a house utterly' -- Niobe: mythological figure whose children were killed by Apollo and Artemis -- House of Pelops: the cursed house of Atreus (Agamemnon, Orestes, etc.) -- Themis: goddess of divine law and order -- 'Suicidal, ruinous, impious': the strongest language Socrates uses to condemn a theological claim -- The principle: human responsibility must not be displaced onto the divine -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (380a-383c), further development of theological principles -- Cross-reference: Euthyphro 6a-c, on whether the gods cause evil

narrative:
"I agree with you," he replied, "and am ready to give my assent to the law."

"Let this then be one of our rules and principles concerning the gods, to which our poets and reciters will be expected to conform -- that God is not the author of all things, but of good only."

"That will do," he said.

"And what do you think of a second principle? Shall I ask you whether God is a magician, and of a nature to appear insidiously now in one shape, and now in another -- sometimes himself changing and passing into many forms, sometimes deceiving us with the semblance of such transformations; or is he one and the same immutably fixed in his own proper image?"

"I cannot answer you," he said, "without more thought."

"Well," I said; "but if we suppose a change in anything, that change must be effected either by the thing itself, or by some other thing?"

"Most certainly."

"And things which are at their best are also least liable to be altered or discomposed; for example, when healthiest and strongest, the human frame is least liable to be affected by meats and drinks, and the plant which is in the fullest vigour also suffers least from winds or the heat of the sun or any similar causes."

"Of course."

"And will not the bravest and wisest soul be least confused or deranged by any external influence?"

"True."

"And the same principle, as I should suppose, applies to all composite things -- furniture, houses, garments; when good and well made, they are least altered by time and circumstances."

"Very true."

"Then everything which is good, whether made by art or nature, or both, is least liable to suffer change from without?"

"True."

"But surely God and the things of God are in every way perfect?"

"Of course they are."

"Then he can hardly be compelled by external influence to take many shapes?"

"He cannot."

"But may he not change and transform himself?"

"Clearly," he said, "that must be the case if he is changed at all."

"And will he then change himself for the better and fairer, or for the worse and more unsightly?"
refashioned:
"I agree," Adeimantus said. "I am ready to make this a law."

"Then here is our first theological principle for poets and performers: God is not the author of all things, but of good only."

"Agreed."

"Now for a second principle. Is God a shapeshifter -- appearing deceptively in different forms, sometimes actually changing, sometimes creating illusions of change? Or is God one and the same, immutably fixed in his own true form?"

"I need to think about that."

"Consider this: any change in something must be caused either by the thing itself or by something external."

"Obviously."

"And things at their peak condition are least affected by external forces. A body at peak health is least disturbed by food and drink. A plant at full vitality is least damaged by wind and sun."

"Of course."

"The bravest and wisest soul is least disturbed by external influences."

"True."

"This applies to all well-made things -- furniture, buildings, clothing. The better crafted they are, the less they change over time."

"Correct."

"So everything that is good, whether natural or crafted, is least subject to change from outside forces."

"True."

"And God and divine things are perfect in every way."

"They are."

"Then God cannot be compelled by external forces to change shape."

"No."

"Could God change himself voluntarily?"

"That would be the only possibility."

"Would such a self-change be for the better or for the worse?"
elegant: The first theological law is established: God is not the author of all things, but of good only. The second inquiry begins: does God change form? Socrates argues that whatever is most perfect is least subject to change -- bodies at peak health, plants at full vigor, well-crafted objects. Since God is perfect, he cannot be changed by external forces. The only remaining possibility is self-change -- but in which direction?
summary: With the first theological principle established (God causes only good), Socrates turns to the second: does God change form? Through a chain of reasoning -- that the more perfect a thing is, the less susceptible it is to change -- he argues that God cannot be changed externally. The question becomes whether God would voluntarily change himself.
distilled: Second theological inquiry: since perfection is least subject to change, and God is perfect, God cannot be altered from without -- could he change himself?
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- This establishes the second tupos (type/principle) of theology: God is unchangeable -- The argument from perfection: what is best is least changeable -- applies universally from bodies to souls to artifacts to God -- 'God is a magician': refers to the many Homeric stories of gods disguising themselves (Zeus as a swan, Athena as Mentor, etc.) -- The question structure (external change vs. self-change) is methodical -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (380d-383c), completion of the second theological type -- Cross-reference: Parmenides fragments on the unchangeability of Being -- Cross-reference: Aristotle, Metaphysics XII (Unmoved Mover) -- This argument prefigures medieval philosophical theology on divine immutability (Aquinas, Summa Theologica I.9)

narrative:
"If he change at all he can only change for the worse, for we cannot suppose him to be deficient either in virtue or beauty."

"Very true, Adeimantus; but then, would any one, whether God or man, desire to make himself worse?"

"Impossible."

"Then it is impossible that God should ever be willing to change; being, as is supposed, the fairest and best that is conceivable, every god remains absolutely and for ever in his own form."

"That necessarily follows," he said, "in my judgment."

"Then," I said, "my dear friend, let none of the poets tell us that

'The gods, taking the disguise of strangers from other lands, walk up and down cities in all sorts of forms;'

and let no one slander Proteus and Thetis, neither let any one, either in tragedy or in any other kind of poetry, introduce Hera disguised in the likeness of a priestess asking an alms --"
refashioned:
"If God changed at all, it could only be for the worse -- since we cannot suppose God is deficient in either virtue or beauty."

"True, Adeimantus. But would anyone -- God or human -- willingly make themselves worse?"

"Impossible."

"Then it is impossible that God would ever willingly change. Being the fairest and best conceivable, every god remains absolutely and forever in his own true form."

"That follows necessarily," he agreed.

"Then let no poet tell us that 'the gods, disguised as strangers from foreign lands, walk through cities in every kind of shape.' Let no one defame Proteus and Thetis with tales of their transformations. Let no one, in tragedy or any other form of poetry, depict Hera disguised as a priestess begging for alms --"
elegant: Since God is already perfect in virtue and beauty, any change could only be for the worse -- and no being willingly degrades itself. Therefore God is absolutely and eternally unchanging. All poetic stories of divine shapeshifting -- gods disguised as strangers, Proteus's transformations, Hera begging in disguise -- must be banned.
summary: Socrates completes the argument for divine immutability: since God is already perfect, any change would be for the worse, and no being willingly becomes worse. Therefore God never changes form. All poetic tales of divine shapeshifting and disguise must be prohibited.
distilled: God, being perfect, can only change for the worse -- but no being willingly degrades itself, so God is eternally unchanging and all stories of divine shapeshifting are false.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- The argument: (1) change from perfection can only be degradation; (2) no being willingly degrades itself; (3) therefore God never changes -- Homer, Odyssey 17.485-487: 'The gods, in the likeness of strangers from other lands, walk through cities in every shape' -- Proteus: the shape-shifting sea god (Odyssey 4.349-570) -- Thetis: sea-nymph, mother of Achilles, known for shapeshifting -- Hera disguised as a priestess: from a lost tragedy, possibly by Aeschylus -- 'Daughters of Inachus': reference continues in 2.54 -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (381b-382a), completion of the divine immutability argument -- Cross-reference: Symposium 211a-b, the Form of Beauty as unchanging -- The divine immutability principle becomes central to later Western theology

narrative:
"'For the life-giving daughters of Inachus the river of Argos;'

-- let us have no more lies of that sort. Neither must we have mothers under the influence of the poets scaring their children with a bad version of these myths -- telling how certain gods, as they say, 'Go about by night in the likeness of so many strangers and in divers forms;' but let them take heed lest they make cowards of their children, and at the same time speak blasphemy against the gods."
refashioned:
"-- 'for the life-giving daughters of Inachus, river of Argos.' No more lies like these.

"And mothers must not be influenced by poets into frightening their children with distorted versions of these myths -- telling them that gods 'wander by night in the guise of strangers and in all manner of shapes.' Such stories make cowards of children while committing blasphemy against the gods."
elegant: Stories of gods wandering in disguise at night must end. Mothers who use such myths to frighten their children simultaneously create cowards and commit blasphemy against the gods.
summary: Socrates condemns the practice of mothers using tales of shape-shifting gods to frighten children, arguing it both produces cowardice and constitutes blasphemy against the divine nature.
distilled: Frightening children with tales of gods in disguise creates cowards and blasphemes the gods simultaneously.
notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Daughters of Inachus': the Argive priestesses; this continues the Hera-as-priestess reference from 2.53 -- Inachus: the river god of Argos, father of Io -- 'Go about by night': Homer, Odyssey 17.485-487 again, now applied to the context of mothers scaring children -- The dual harm: psychological damage to children (cowardice) and theological error (blasphemy) -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (381d-e), completing the ban on stories of divine transformation -- The practical dimension: Plato is concerned not just with elite poetry but with everyday storytelling in the household -- Mothers and nurses are identified as primary vectors of harmful mythology

narrative:
"Heaven forbid," he said.

"But although the gods are themselves unchangeable, still by witchcraft and deception they may make us think that they appear in various forms?"

"Perhaps," he replied.

"Well, but can you imagine that God will be willing to lie, whether in word or deed, or to put forth a phantom of himself?"

"I cannot say," he replied.

"Do you not know," I said, "that the true lie, if such an expression may be allowed, is hated of gods and men?"

"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean that no one is willingly deceived in that which is the truest and highest part of himself, or about the truest and highest matters; there, above all, he is most afraid of a lie having possession of him."

"Still," he said, "I do not comprehend you."

"The reason is," I replied, "that you attribute some profound meaning to my words; but I am only saying that deception, or being deceived or uninformed about the highest realities in the highest part of themselves, which is the soul, and in that part of them to have and to hold the lie, is what mankind least like -- that, I say, is what they utterly detest."

"There is nothing more hateful to them."

"And, as I was just now remarking, this ignorance in the soul of him who is deceived may be called the true lie; for the lie in words is only a kind of imitation and shadowy image of a previous affection of the soul, not pure unadulterated falsehood. Am I not right?"

"Perfectly right."

"The true lie is hated not only by the gods, but also by men?"
refashioned:
"Heaven forbid," Adeimantus said.

"But even if the gods themselves do not actually change, could they use illusion or deception to make us think they appear in different forms?"

"Perhaps."

"But can you imagine God willingly lying -- in word, in deed, or by projecting a false image of himself?"

"I am not sure."

"Consider this: do you not know that the 'true lie' -- if I may use that phrase -- is hated by both gods and men?"

"What do you mean by 'true lie'?"

"I mean that no one wants to be deceived about the most important things in the most important part of themselves. What people fear most is having a lie take hold of their soul regarding the highest realities."

"I still do not follow."

"I am saying something quite simple: to be deceived or ignorant about the highest truths in the highest part of yourself -- the soul -- and to harbor falsehood there, is the thing human beings hate most in the world. They absolutely detest it."

"Nothing is more hateful to them."

"This ignorance lodged in the deceived person's soul is what I call the 'true lie.' A lie in words is merely an imitation, a shadowy copy of the deeper falsehood already residing in the soul. The verbal lie is not the real falsehood -- it is a secondary reflection."

"That is exactly right."

"And this true lie -- falsehood in the soul -- is hated by gods and men alike?"
elegant: Socrates introduces the crucial distinction between the 'true lie' and the 'lie in words.' The true lie is falsehood lodged in the soul -- being deceived about the highest realities in one's deepest self. This is universally and utterly detested by both gods and men. Verbal lies are merely secondary shadows of this deeper soul-deception. Could God, who is unchanging, willingly deceive us even through illusion?
summary: Socrates distinguishes the 'true lie' -- falsehood residing in the soul about the highest realities -- from the mere 'lie in words,' which is only a secondary reflection. The true lie is what both gods and men hate most. This distinction addresses whether God could deceive through illusion even without actually changing form.
distilled: The 'true lie' is falsehood lodged in the soul about the highest realities -- universally detested by gods and men alike, far worse than any verbal deception.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'True lie' (alethos pseudos) vs. 'lie in words' (en tois logois pseudos): one of the Republic's most important philosophical distinctions -- The true lie = ignorance in the soul about ultimate realities; the verbal lie = a mere reflection or imitation of soul-falsehood -- This distinction parallels the Republic's metaphysics: reality vs. appearance, Forms vs. images -- Cross-reference: Republic Book V (476d-480a), knowledge vs. opinion -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VI (508d-509b), the Sun analogy and the Good as the source of truth -- Cross-reference: Republic Book VII (514a-521b), the Cave allegory -- 'The highest part of themselves, which is the soul': prefigures the tripartite soul with reason as its highest element -- The distinction between true and verbal lies becomes crucial for understanding the 'noble lie' (414b-415d)

narrative:
"Yes."

"Whereas the lie in words is in certain cases useful and not hateful; in dealing with enemies -- that would be an instance; or again, when those whom we call our friends in a fit of madness or illusion are going to do some harm, then it is useful and is a sort of medicine or preventive; also in the tales of mythology, of which we were just now speaking -- because we do not know the truth about ancient times, we make falsehood as much like truth as we can, and so turn it to account."
refashioned:
"Yes."

"But verbal lies, unlike the true lie, can sometimes be useful and not hateful. Against enemies, for example -- deception in warfare is legitimate. Or when friends, in a fit of madness or delusion, are about to do something harmful -- then a lie serves as medicine or prevention. And in mythology, which we were just discussing: since we do not know the truth about ancient times, we make our fictional accounts as close to truth as possible, and put them to good use."
elegant: While the true lie in the soul is always hateful, verbal lies have legitimate uses: deceiving enemies, preventing harm from delusional friends (as a kind of medicine), and crafting mythology about unknown ancient events to approximate truth as closely as possible.
summary: Socrates identifies three legitimate uses of verbal lies: deceiving enemies, preventing harm from delusional friends (as a form of medicine), and constructing mythology about ancient times whose truth is unknown -- making fiction as truth-like as possible.
distilled: Verbal lies, unlike soul-falsehood, can be useful: against enemies, as medicine for delusional friends, and in mythology approximating unknown truths.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Three legitimate uses of verbal falsehood: (1) deception of enemies, (2) therapeutic deception of deranged friends, (3) mythological approximation of unknown history -- 'Medicine or preventive' (pharmakon): the medicinal lie foreshadows the 'noble lie' of Republic Book III (414b-415d) -- 'Make falsehood as much like truth as we can': the criterion for acceptable mythology -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (389b-c), where lying is permitted to rulers as medicine for the state -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (414b-415d), the Noble Lie (Phoenician tale) -- Cross-reference: Hippias Minor 365b-372a, Socrates' paradoxical argument that the voluntary liar is better than the involuntary one -- The distinction anticipates modern debates about justified deception, 'white lies,' and propaganda

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"But can any of these reasons apply to God? Can we suppose that he is ignorant of antiquity, and therefore has recourse to invention?"

"That would be ridiculous," he said.

"Then the lying poet has no place in our idea of God?"

"I should say not."

"Or perhaps he may tell a lie because he is afraid of enemies?"

"That is inconceivable."

"But he may have friends who are senseless or mad?"

"But no mad or senseless person can be a friend of God."

"Then no motive can be imagined why God should lie?"

"None whatever."

"Then the superhuman and divine is absolutely incapable of falsehood?"

"Yes."

"Then is God perfectly simple and true both in word and deed; he changes not; he deceives not, either by sign or word, by dream or waking vision."

"Your thoughts," he said, "are the reflection of my own."

"You agree with me then," I said, "that this is the second type or form in which we should write and speak about divine things. The gods are not magicians who transform themselves, neither do they deceive mankind in any way."

"I grant that."

"Then, although we are admirers of Homer, we do not admire the lying dream which Zeus sends to Agamemnon; neither will we praise the verses of Aeschylus in which Thetis says that Apollo at her nuptials --"
refashioned:
"True," Adeimantus said.

"But do any of these justifications for lying apply to God? Is God ignorant about ancient events and therefore forced to invent?"

"That is absurd."

"So God has no need for poetic invention. Does God lie out of fear of enemies?"

"Inconceivable."

"Does God have friends who are mad or delusional and need therapeutic deception?"

"No mad or senseless person can be a friend of God."

"Then there is no conceivable reason for God to lie?"

"None at all."

"Then the divine nature is absolutely incapable of falsehood."

"Yes."

"God is perfectly simple and truthful in both word and deed. He does not change. He does not deceive -- not by signs, words, dreams, or waking visions."

"My thoughts exactly," Adeimantus said.

"Then this is our second theological principle: the gods are not shapeshifters who transform themselves, nor do they deceive human beings in any way."

"I agree."

"So even though we admire Homer, we reject the false dream Zeus sends to Agamemnon. And we will not praise the verses of Aeschylus in which Thetis says that Apollo at her wedding --"
elegant: None of the legitimate reasons for lying apply to God: he is not ignorant, not fearful, and has no mad friends needing therapeutic deception. Therefore God is absolutely incapable of falsehood -- perfectly simple and true in word and deed, unchanging, never deceiving by any means. This second theological principle bans all stories of divine deception, including Zeus's false dream sent to Agamemnon and Apollo's false prophecies to Thetis.
summary: Socrates eliminates every possible justification for divine lying and establishes the second theological principle: God is absolutely truthful, unchanging, and incapable of deception in any form. This requires rejecting Homer's story of Zeus sending a false dream to Agamemnon and Aeschylus's account of Apollo's broken promises to Thetis.
distilled: God has no reason to lie and is absolutely incapable of falsehood -- the second theological principle: the gods neither change form nor deceive in any way.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- Second tupos (theological type): God is unchanging and truthful -- 'Perfectly simple and true': divine simplicity and truthfulness become foundational concepts in later theology -- Zeus's lying dream: Iliad 2.1-34, where Zeus sends a false dream to Agamemnon promising victory -- Aeschylus, from a lost play (probably the lost Bassarai or another work about Thetis): Thetis accuses Apollo of prophesying falsely about her son Achilles -- 'By sign or word, by dream or waking vision': a comprehensive list covering every mode of possible divine communication -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (382c-383c), where the two types of theology are summarized -- Cross-reference: Timaeus 29e, where the Demiurge is described as free from jealousy and deception -- The two theological types together: (1) God causes only good; (2) God is unchanging and truthful

narrative:
Socrates quoted Aeschylus, in which Thetis says of Apollo: "He was celebrating in song her fair progeny whose days were to be long, and to know no sickness. And when he had spoken of my lot as in all things blessed of heaven he raised a note of triumph and cheered my soul.

"And I thought that the word of Phoebus being divine and full of prophecy, would not fail. And now he himself who uttered the strain, he who was present at the banquet, and who said this -- he it is who has slain my son."
refashioned:
Socrates quoted Aeschylus, in which Thetis accuses Apollo: "He sang of my beautiful children -- how they would live long lives free from sickness. He described my fate as blessed by heaven in every way, and raised a song of triumph that filled my heart with joy.

"I trusted that the words of Phoebus Apollo, being divine and filled with prophetic truth, could not fail. And yet it is he himself -- the one who sang that song, who sat at my wedding banquet, who made those promises -- it is he who has killed my son."
elegant: In Aeschylus's lost play, Thetis accuses Apollo of the cruelest deception: at her wedding he prophesied that her children would live long, healthy, blessed lives. She trusted his divine word completely. Yet Apollo himself killed her son Achilles. The god who promised happiness delivered death.
summary: Socrates quotes Aeschylus's devastating accusation by Thetis against Apollo: the god prophesied at her wedding that her children would enjoy long, blessed lives, then himself killed her son Achilles -- the ultimate example of divine deception.
distilled: Thetis accuses Apollo of the worst divine lie: promising her son a blessed life at her wedding, then killing him.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Aeschylus, from a lost play (possibly the Psychostasia or a work in the Achilles trilogy): Thetis's lament for Achilles -- 'Phoebus' = Apollo, god of prophecy, music, and the sun -- Thetis: sea-nymph, mother of Achilles; her wedding to Peleus was attended by the gods -- Apollo's role in Achilles' death: in the Iliad, Apollo guides Paris's arrow to kill Achilles -- The accusation is devastating: the god of truth and prophecy is presented as the ultimate deceiver -- This is precisely the kind of story Plato's second theological principle would ban: a god who deceives and then destroys -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (383a-c), where this passage is cited as the culmination of the censorship argument -- The emotional power of Thetis's accusation makes it the most compelling example of why such stories are dangerous

narrative:
"These are the kind of sentiments about the gods which will arouse our anger; and he who utters them shall be refused a chorus; neither shall we allow teachers to make use of them in the instruction of the young, meaning, as we do, that our guardians, as far as men can be, should be true worshippers of the gods and like them."

"I entirely agree," he said, "in these principles, and promise to make them my laws."
refashioned:
"These are exactly the kind of claims about the gods that will provoke our anger. Any poet who makes them will be denied a chorus for their plays. We will not allow teachers to use such stories in educating the young. Our intention is that the guardians should be, as far as humanly possible, true worshippers of the gods and godlike themselves."

"I completely agree with these principles," Adeimantus said, "and I pledge to make them my laws."
elegant: Poets who misrepresent the gods will be denied the right to produce their plays. Teachers may not use such stories. The goal: guardians who are true worshippers of the gods and, as far as humanly possible, godlike themselves. Adeimantus pledges to make these principles law.
summary: Socrates concludes Book II's theological argument: poets who misrepresent the gods will be censored, teachers forbidden from using such stories, and guardians raised to be true worshippers who aspire to godlikeness. Adeimantus fully endorses these principles as law.
distilled: Poets who misrepresent the gods will be banned, teachers restricted, and guardians raised as true worshippers who strive to be godlike.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus in dialogue -- 'Refused a chorus': in Athens, dramatic productions required a wealthy sponsor (choregus) and official approval; denying a chorus meant the play could not be performed at public festivals -- This is practical censorship: controlling the means of artistic production -- 'True worshippers of the gods and like them': the guardians' education aims at homoiosis theoi (becoming like god), a concept central to Platonic ethics -- 'Promise to make them my laws': Adeimantus's pledge signals the transition from theoretical discussion to practical legislation -- Cross-reference: Republic Book III (386a-403c), where censorship guidelines continue in detail -- Cross-reference: Republic Book X (595a-608b), the final reckoning with poetry -- Cross-reference: Theaetetus 176a-b, 'becoming like God as far as possible' (homoiosis theoi kata to dunaton) -- This concludes Book II; Book III continues with specific censorship of Homer, Hesiod, and the tragedians -- The two theological types established: (1) God is the cause of good only; (2) God is unchanging and truthful
